items: Daily Digest and Weekly Digest each get a Configure button, opening a drawer that mirrors the Morning one with two controls: on/off and send time (Europe/Helsinki). No AI, no sections, no recipient control; recipients stay admins and the cadence (daily every day except Friday, weekly on Friday) is unchanged.

The scheduler reads these through a new digestCfg persisted in settings.design.digestDaily and digestWeekly; a disabled digest stops sending and a changed time takes effect on the next tick. The digest email builder is untouched, so the emails are byte-identical.

Clicking any activity bar on the Schedule Gantt now opens a scheduling narrative drawer: a plain-language summary, the planned dates with baseline variance, the reschedule history from the activity reschedule log, the predecessors it is waiting on with the binding one flagged, the downstream activities it would push if it slips further, and any open constraints.

Every line is computed from stored data (reschedule log, predecessors via the forward pass, saved baseline, constraint register); nothing is inferred or AI generated. Activities with no reschedules recorded in DLP say so rather than invent a history, and baseline variance shows only when a baseline is saved.
The previous jump to the board to edit is preserved as a button inside the drawer. Calendar and Workload drill panels are unchanged.

Digest scheduler skew guard. The daily and weekly admin digests and the Morning Cx Update send from a browser tab, so a tab left open across a deploy could send with stale bundled code (a daily digest landed uncentred this way). Before sending a due boundary the scheduler now confirms the tab is on the current deploy, and if a newer one has shipped it reloads once and lets the 72 hour catch-up resend on the fresh build.
The deployed changelog head is the build stamp; the check is cache-busted and fails open, so a read miss never blocks a send. No change to any email content, layout, logo, font, or size.

Invite and sign-in-link emails are now centred on a light page, widened to 600, and carry the atnorth identity: a navy masthead with the project logo (pulled from project branding, the same source as the weekly report) and a navy footer band.
The old in-card 'Sent by ... CSN Commissioning' sign-off line is folded into the footer band, which reads 'Delivered by DLP, Digital Last Planner' and an invite-specific validity note.

The logo appears on project-scoped sends (project invites and member re-sends) and falls back to an 'atnorth' wordmark, then to no logo, when no project branding is in scope (for example a bare Global Contacts invite).

Project team panel: the header block (Resend and Export buttons, search and filters, and the column headers) now stays pinned while the member rows scroll beneath, matching the pinned pattern used on the other list panels.
Previously only the filters pinned while the column headers scrolled out of view; the whole header block is now wrapped in a single sticky container defined in the always-present main stylesheet.
All three app emails (Morning Cx Update, Weekly Report, admin digests) now sit centred on a light page at 780 wide, matching the Morning Cx Update width.
Weekly Report widened from 600 to 780 and digest from 620 to 780; inner layouts reflow (all inner tables were already full-width or percentage-based).

Added a footer band to each email. Morning and Weekly (project team) carry no logo and no commissioning-team wording: 'Delivered by DLP, Digital Last Planner' and 'You receive this as a member of the [project] project team.'
Digest footer band carries QMC branding.
Folded the three existing in-card sign-off lines (morning 'Sent automatically', weekly 'CSN Commissioning', digest 'Automated update') into the new bands so the sign-off lives in one place.

Shared emailShell/projectFooter/qmcFooter helpers added to digestCore and imported by morningReport and outlook. Note: grey surround shows in web clients and degrades to a centred card on white in classic Outlook.

A witnessed activity can no longer be marked Complete while its witness outcome is still Pending. In the activity editor, setting Status to Complete on an activity with a witness invite now blocks Save until the Witness Outcome is Succeeded or Failed, with the reason shown in the Still needed line. A visual aid makes it clear: the Witness Outcome control is outlined in red and a red callout appears explaining what to do. The outcome control stays usable after Complete is selected so the required outcome can be recorded, which also fixes a latent trap where marking Complete locked the row and would have stopped a non-admin from ever recording the outcome.

Invite Type is now a managed per-project list, matching the Systems pattern. Owners and admins can add a new invite type inline from the dropdown in the activity editor (a plus Add new type option) and it persists into the dropdown for the whole project; there is also an Invite Types tab under Project Setup to add, rename and delete types, with a guard that blocks deleting a type still in use by an activity. Backed by a new invite_types table with a composite (project_id, name) primary key, so the shared vocabulary (W2, L2 FOK and the rest) can exist in every project, unlike systems which has a name only key. Seeded with the previous eight defaults for every existing project. The accompanying SQL migration must be applied in Supabase before this code goes live.

Morning Cx Update executive summary tightened for concise, actionable bullets, and the token ceiling raised so it can no longer cut off. In the super-action edge function the morning system prompt now requires one line and one idea per bullet, five to seven bullets (eight maximum), each led by the action or the owner who must act (chase, resolve, confirm, push, escalate) and capped at about one sentence; and the morning max_tokens is raised from 600 to 900 so a busy day never truncates the summary mid sentence. This is an edge function change: it takes effect only when the function is redeployed in Supabase, not on a Vercel push. The live deployed function may differ from this repo copy, so apply the two changes (the morning system prompt and the max_tokens value) into the live function rather than replacing it wholesale.

Right hand date and status labels in the Morning Cx Update no longer wrap. The constraint due dates (for example was needed 22 Jun) and the Today, Tomorrow and Overdue status labels are now rendered with non-breaking spaces and white-space nowrap, so each stays on a single line regardless of email width. This was a wrap problem in the date cell, not an overall width problem: long constraint descriptions on the left always compete for space, so widening alone would not have fixed it. Descriptions still wrap normally; only the short right hand labels are protected.

Executive summary panel restyled (Option A). The pale blue fill is removed for a clean white background with a single 4px navy left rule (matching the masthead) and a navy EXECUTIVE SUMMARY kicker and bullets. The summary now reads through its heading and edge rule rather than a coloured wash, so it no longer competes with the dark masthead directly above it.

Follow up to REV303. Classic Outlook converts CSS pixel font sizes to points at a 0.75 ratio (96px equals 72pt), so the pixel values left in the email were rendering at roughly 8 to 10pt and looked shrunken, and nested table cells lost the size they were meant to inherit. Every text cell now carries an explicit point size and the whole email is in points: body reading text (activity rows, executive summary, constraints, daily updates and witness rows) at 12pt, section headers at 14pt, right side status labels at 11pt, meta and small labels at 10pt, masthead micro type at 9pt and KPI numbers at 15pt. Nothing renders below 9pt, so what you set in code is what the Outlook ribbon shows.

The Morning Cx Update email now reads at the native Outlook size. Body reading text (executive summary, activity names and section rows) moves from 13px to 12pt, section headers to 13pt and the right side status labels to 11pt, so the message no longer looks shrunken next to a normal Outlook email. The whole email is set in the Aptos font stack (Aptos first, then Segoe UI, Calibri and Arial for anyone outside Microsoft 365), matching the current Office default. Font family is forced on each text cell rather than relying on inheritance, which classic Outlook drops into nested tables. The body is widened from 720px to 780px so the larger type does not crowd the executive summary. The shared Open the board button and the daily and weekly digests are unchanged.

Clearing a constraint now asks for confirmation. From every place a constraint can be cleared (the activity card, the notification box, the constraints table and the activity editor) a prompt appears: only proceed if you are sure the constraint has been cleared. Reopening a constraint is not gated. A single shared guard sits in front of all four paths so the confirmation cannot be missed on one of them.

The Morning Cx Update email is wider (720px) and its type is now on a consistent scale. Font sizes had drifted across eight near-duplicate values; they are consolidated into a clear hierarchy: larger section headers, more readable body and activity rows, a single small-label size, and aligned masthead and KPI sizes. The result is less cramped and easier to scan.

The Morning Cx Update summary now lets the AI drive the formatting, and defaults to scannable bullets. The summary had been borrowing the weekly client report's prompt, which is written for flowing prose and actively discouraged lists, so a request for bullets produced a wall of text. It now has its own dedicated prompt built for a fast field brief: bullets by default, the author's instruction leading, blockers first. The email is also wider (700px). The AI formatting change lives in the super-action function, so it must be redeployed to take effect.

Two fixes to the Morning Cx Update email. It is wider now (640px instead of 560), so the content is less cramped. And your AI instruction is actually respected: the summary was being forced into paragraphs by a hardcoded instruction that overrode an explicit bullet request, so a prompt asking for bullet points was ignored. Now, if your prompt asks for bullets you get tight bullet lines, and with no prompt it defaults to scannable bullets suited to a quick morning read; plain prose still renders as paragraphs. The bullet formatting requires the super-action function to be redeployed to take full effect.

The Morning Cx AI instruction box is now clearly persistent. It already saved when you clicked away, and now there is an explicit Save prompt button and a brief Saved confirmation, so there is no doubt the wording is stored. The saved prompt is reused every morning until you edit it and save again. The box also re-seeds from the stored value, so it always shows what is actually saved.

Fixed the Morning Cx Update configuration not saving: toggles like Admins only reverted moments after clicking. The config was being written to a place that was never persisted to the database, so the next background refresh restored the defaults. It now saves alongside the design settings, which do persist, so recipients, days, sections, send time and AI instructions all stick. Any config set before this fix will need setting once more, since the earlier values were never stored.

Vendor logos now show in the report preview too. The sent email carries each vendor logo as an attachment; the preview now maps each one back to the company's hosted image (using the same key the mailer uses), so contractor logos render in the preview instead of being hidden. Any logo that genuinely has no hosted image is still hidden rather than shown broken.

The QMC logo in the report preview now matches the background it sits on. Older archived digests have a white header and newer ones a dark band; the preview picks the dark-ink logo for white headers and the light logo for the dark band, so the mark is always visible rather than washing out.

The report preview in Reports no longer shows a broken QMC logo. Sent emails carry their logos as attachments, which the in-app preview could not resolve, so it drew a broken-image box; the preview now points the QMC mark at its hosted image and quietly hides any other attachment-only images, so it looks like the delivered email.

Fixed a crash on the Documentation Tracker: clicking any of the L1 to L5 tag cards threw an error and dropped you to the recovery panel. The card was setting the level filter to a single text value, but that filter became a multi-select (which expects a list) in an earlier revision, so the control broke as soon as a card set it. The cards now set the filter correctly and select or clear the matching level as intended.

The Send to Board dialog is wider so its table fits without a horizontal scrollbar. It was capped at 920px, which truncated the assignee column and forced a scroll for the sake of a few columns; it now opens at 1280px (still capped to the screen on narrow displays), so every column, including the full assignee email, is visible at once.

Removed two vestigial sub-labels from the planning board metric strip: the 'N within Nd' line under Need make-ready and the 'predecessor knock-on' line under At risk. The metrics themselves, their counts and their click-through drills are unchanged; only the small grey captions that were peeking above the week header are gone.

The weekly admin digest now shows the week number in its header chip (WK 27 rather than a generic WEEKLY tag); the daily digest keeps its DAILY chip. The number is the ISO week of the period end, matching the weekly report email.

The QMC mark in the admin digest header now uses a light version made for the dark band, so it sits inside the header instead of on a white tile. A dark-background logo (white mark on a transparent ground) is stored alongside the existing one and the digest picks it up automatically, falling back to the original if it is not present. The updated QMC artwork supplied is now the stored logo.

The internal admin digests (daily and weekly) get the modern header from the mockup. The plain white header becomes a dark QMC band carrying the Quantum Mission Critical logo, a kicker (DAILY or WEEKLY UPDATE, ADMIN), the FIN04 DLP headline and a mono kind chip, with the four-colour rule sitting flush beneath. Same structural language as the atnorth stakeholder reports so the whole email suite reads as one family, while the QMC logo keeps the internal digests distinct.

Two touches on the weekly report email. The header now leads with the week number (Week 28, week ending...) rather than just the end date. The four KPI toppers get their colours back: the coloured accent bars above PPC, Delayed, Witness Passed and Make-Ready were relying on CSS variables that email clients drop, so they all rendered dark; they now use literal colours (house blue, red, green and amber) that Outlook and Gmail keep.

Three follow-ups from the mockup review. The weekly report EMAIL (the one with the two HTML attachments) now wears the same atnorth identity masthead as the Morning Cx Update, with the week and generated-by in the header and the blue accent rule beneath; its coloured KPI toppers are unchanged. The Morning Cx Update now carries the actual atnorth logo when the brand has one set (the logo guard was loosened to accept the hosted, data and content-id forms that the invite pipeline already uses), and the atnorth commissioning line is gone from its footer.

The Morning Cx Update email gets the matching identity from the mockup: the flat blue bar becomes the atnorth dark band with a small kicker over the project name, a monospace project chip and date on the right, and a thin solid blue rule beneath (a table row, so Outlook keeps it). A signed atnorth line closes the footer. The logo is shown only when the brand has a hosted image; otherwise a clean atnorth wordmark stands in, so the masthead can never render a broken image in Outlook or Gmail. The internal admin digests keep their existing Quantum Mission Critical header, since those are internal QMC tool digests rather than the stakeholder-facing morning email.

The weekly report masthead gets a stronger identity (the refined direction from the mockup). An uppercase kicker sits over a larger, tighter headline (the week number, or Lookahead for a custom range), with the commencing and lookahead dates on a clean line beneath; the issue details move under a monospace project-and-week reference chip. The atnorth dark band and its status-colour accent stripe stay. The footer is now signed with the atnorth mark. Same footprint, a more composed document; the email templates follow in the next revision.

Buttons now show a keyboard focus ring and a press state. Every button built on the shared style (which is almost all of them, the board toggles you already like, dialog actions, table controls and the rest) now draws a clear accent outline when reached by keyboard, matching the ring inputs already had, and dips slightly when pressed for tactile feedback. The ring is keyboard-only, so mouse clicks stay clean. The multi-select filter buttons get the same focus treatment. This closes the keyboard-accessibility gap the audit flagged without changing how anything looks under the mouse.

Colour-token cleanup: the danger red is now a theme variable. Roughly seventy interface surfaces that had the red hardcoded (failure chips, overdue card borders, the fail badge, destructive-action buttons, late-ticket tints and more) now read it from a single --red token, defined to the exact shade they used before so the default theme looks identical, pixel for pixel. The practical gain: the high-contrast and colour-blind palettes now recolour these surfaces too, where before they were left behind. SVG chart marks, email templates and gradient fills keep their literal colour by design, since CSS variables are unreliable in those contexts.

Two tidy-ups on the Activity Table and Constraints Log. On the Table, the Start date and Witness time no longer wrap onto two lines in narrow columns; each stays on a single line. The Constraints Log now respects the same 1500px content width as every other page instead of stretching edge to edge on wide monitors, the last page that was missing the cap.

The AI narrative function is no longer an open endpoint. super-action, which spends Anthropic credits to write the weekly and morning summaries, now requires a valid signed-in user before it does any work, using the same session check the admin-users function uses; a caller holding only the public site key is turned away with an unauthorised response before any API call is made. The app's own calls are unaffected because they already carry the signed-in session. This must be deployed to the super-action edge function to take effect; the repository copy is now the gated version.

Page-level error boundary. A render error inside any page (the board, the table, analytics, assets, docs, admin and the rest) used to blank the entire app; now it is caught and shown as a small recoverable panel while the sidebar, project switcher and Help stay live. Switching to another page clears the error automatically, and the panel offers a Try again and a Reload. This closes the white-screen failure mode structurally: the boot self-test already guarded start-up, and this guards everything after it.

Email button fix, take two. The rounded VML button did not survive the journey: mail sent through Microsoft Graph has its HTML sanitised and the Outlook-conditional markup arrived mangled, clipping the label. The shared button now uses the padded-cell pattern the witness invites have always rendered correctly with: padding on the cell so Outlook honours it, rounded corners where supported, and a label that cannot clip. The executive summary header also drops its AI attribution tag.

Morning Cx Update, second generation. The email now opens with an AI-written executive summary in short paragraphs: it names blockers, calls out late activities that are pushing their successors through the predecessor links, and flags tomorrow's at-risk starts before they slip; a new AI-instructions box in the morning configuration tailors its tone and emphasis any time, figures always come from that morning's data, and the email sends normally if the AI is unreachable. The body gains a Yesterday / Today / Tomorrow structure with the previous day's daily updates quoted inline, and constraint lines now carry an owner or a red unowned mark plus what each constraint blocks. Every button in every DLP email, the morning update, admin digests, witness invites and asset notifications, is rebuilt on the bulletproof pattern so Outlook renders a proper rounded button instead of a text-hugging box.

Correction to yesterday's masthead fix: the report's header band is dark in both themes (the light report is a light sheet under the same dark masthead), so keying the logo on the report theme handed the light report the wrong variant. The masthead now always prefers the dark-background logo slot, in both themes, with the white-chip treatment whenever only a light asset is available. Upload the dark logo under Branding and both reports carry it natively.

The weekly report masthead now picks the logo for its theme: the dark report takes the brand's dark-background logo, the light report the light one, each falling back to the other slot before the built-in image. If the dark report has only a light-background asset to work with, the logo sits on a small white chip instead of washing out against the dark header. Configure both logo slots under Branding for the cleanest result.

Multi-select filters, tranche 4 of 4, the series complete. The platform directory on the hub (Company with No-company, Platform role where Supers still covers the owner, Invite state), Project Team members (Company, Role, Status as any-of active or pending), the Audit user filter (now server-side: the paginated query filters on the whole selected set, and the presence popout's Audit button lands as a one-person selection), and Analytics (Company, Building, Cx Stage, with the report's summary sheet listing every selected value). The hub control carries a light palette so it matches the hub's theme rather than assuming the app's dark variables. Every filter dropdown in the app now takes any combination; value-editing dropdowns remain single-choice by design.

Multi-select filters, tranche 3: Asset Status (Hall, Level, Type, and Status where the three modes combine as any-of: overdue, in progress, not started; the clickable KPI tiles now toggle membership in that set) and the Documentation Tracker (Level, Responsible party, Vendor). The Docs level filter's stage highlight follows when exactly one level is chosen and steps aside for combinations. The Docs Status select stays native: it has one real option (Has outstanding), and a one-checkbox popover would be worse than the toggle it effectively is. Remaining: witness, team, audit and analytics filters.

Multi-select filters, tranche 2: the Activity Table (Company including its No-company option, Building, Cx Stage, Status) and the Constraints Log (Company, Building) now take any combination through the shared control. Saved table views upgrade in place: a view saved with the old single-choice filters loads as the equivalent selection, and views saved from now on keep the arrays. One correction to the announced plan: the Planning Board has no filter dropdowns (search and week navigation only), so this tranche pairs the Table with Constraints. Remaining: Asset Status and Docs, then witness, team, audit and analytics filters.

The boot smoke test lands, the structural answer to tonight's white screens. A script boots the real app bundle in a headless browser DOM against a generated stub of the data layer, with a fake session and an auto-entered project, and asserts three frames: module evaluation (where the first crash lived), the loading frame, and the full board past every loading-screen early return (where the second crash lived). It was proven the honest way: re-introducing tonight's bug made it fail with the exact error users saw, and the current code passes. It now runs in the delivery gate suite for every revision that touches the app shell.

Multi-select filters, tranche 1 of 4. A shared filter control lands (summary button, instant-apply checkbox popover, Select all clears back to All, Esc or click-away closes, active filters get the accent border) and Benchmarks converts first: Disciplines and Statuses now take any combination. Show Completed remains the master switch; ticking Completed in the status filter narrows within what the toggle allows and never resurrects hidden rows. Value-editing dropdowns across the app deliberately stay single-choice. Remaining tranches: Activity Table and Board, then Asset Status and Docs, then witness, team, audit and analytics filters.

Presence popout layout rework. The rail's generic button styling (40px squares collapsed, full-width rows expanded) was restyling the popout's Audit buttons, overlapping names in the expanded rail and drifting rows in the collapsed one; those buttons now carry their own fully-reset style at higher specificity, the same defence the Display flyout has always used. Rows are a rigid avatar / text / button grid that cannot wrap-collide: names ellipsise with the full name on hover, timestamps are short and never wrap (full timestamp on hover), and the latest-activity line keeps its single ellipsised line.

Third and final fix in the presence saga: clicking the online popout white-paged because its activity loader referenced projectId, which only exists as a parameter of an inner function, not in the component's scope; the crash waited for the first click because the effect body only runs when the popout opens. It now uses selProj, the component's actual selected-project binding, the effect body is defensively wrapped so a failure degrades to empty activity lines instead of a crash, and the positional proof in the patch pipeline now also covers this binding. The presence feature is functional end to end.

Hotfix of the hotfix, structural this time. REV266 moved the presence hooks below the app's loading-screen early returns, so the moment loading finished React saw more hooks than the loading frame had and crashed: the one second of Loading, then white. The hooks now live at the top of the component above every early return, per the REV249 doctrine already written in the code for exactly this trap, with the admin gate computed from bindings that provably exist there (the same formula cu.role itself uses). The patch pipeline now proves, positionally, that every binding the inserted code reads is declared above it and that no presence hook sits below the first early return.

Hotfix: REV265 crashed the app on load (white page) because the presence polling effect referenced isAdmin in its dependency list before that variable was declared; dependency lists are evaluated during render, so every load hit a reference error. The two presence effects now live after the declaration and the feature works as designed. Nothing else changed.

Who is online, on the rail (admins and the owner). The collapsed rail shows a people icon with a live count; the expanded rail shows a face pile with the online count. Clicking either opens the presence popout: online-now and earlier sections, each person with their most recent audit entry in plain words and an Audit button that opens Admin > Audit filtered to them; the footer opens the full log. Presence polls every 30 seconds; the activity lines load once per open.

FIN3021 readiness: the FIN04-shaped surfaces are de-hardcoded ahead of the second project going live. The Documentation and Asset Status Excel exports, the Energised notification subject and the RFFE subject and PDF name all take the current project's name (registered once from Branding, no prop-threading). The weekly report masthead uses the project's configured logo with the atnorth image as legacy fallback, and its alt text follows the project. The asset tag browse list and auto-derivation are FIN04-only, since the bundled register is Koski's equipment; other projects keep manual tags cleanly. The witness contact resolver gates itself by project: on any project without a configured matrix it returns nobody and names itself unconfigured, so a Vantaa invite can never silently summon the Koski contact set.

The Display colour palettes now reach everywhere status is shown. Around fifty places coloured status by fixed hex (the constrained amber, the added/starts green, the done teal, the overdue red, and their tinted backgrounds) across the board, YTT badges, witness schedule, audit chips, admin pending markers, report tiles and legends; they now colour through a small status-variable family whose defaults are byte-identical to before, so the default palette renders exactly as it did, while High Contrast and Colourblind finally recolour these surfaces. Solid badges gain per-palette text colours, so High Contrast's darker fills carry white text and remain readable. Deliberately unchanged: the house-blue ongoing badge, the palette picker's own swatches, and two theme-tuned shades noted for a follow-up.

Help overhaul, tranche 4, the structural finish. Every page now names its help topic in a checked map beside the page registry, and the Help button is smart: it lands on the topic for the page you came from (a member is never landed on an admin-gated topic). An unmapped page announces itself instead of staying silently undocumented.

The coverage check immediately caught its second orphan: the Documentation Tracker had no help topic. It now has one, covering the equipment-type by document matrix, the SharePoint-fed workbook source, Codes mode and the short-code vocabulary, responsible parties, overrides that survive the sync, per-vendor targets and the Excel export.

Witness invites: the required invite type is now documented with its verified option list (L2 FOK, W2, IVC, L3 FOK, L3 SU, L3 SAT, L4 FPT, L5 IST) and where it travels: subject, body, updates, retests and the schedule pills.

Help overhaul, tranche 3: drift corrections. The audit documentation now matches the current page: Undo and Force revert (the buttons were renamed some time ago but help still taught the old names), plus the day grouping, category chips, server-side full-history search and Export all. Marking work complete now documents the percent rule (admins any activity, members their own company's, client viewers none). Constraints & make-ready mentions the YTT quick-add path for raising a blocker at the stand-up.

Help overhaul, tranche 2: the appearance systems get documented. Your Display settings (Getting started, everyone) explains the per-person palette choices, what the three palettes actually recolour and where, the light and dark theme toggle, and the PPC readout. Design & appearance (Administration, admins) covers the project-wide Admin Design tab: global versus per-page scope, all seven sections (Icons with the full library, Colour, Typography, Layout content widths and which pages honour them, Board options, Naming, Effects with depth and reduced motion), and the boundary with Branding.
Refreshing while on Admin's Design tab now stays there; the tab-restore list was missing the design entry and bounced to Companies.

Help overhaul, tranche 1. The Benchmarks page gets its first help topic (Benchmarks & the FOK register, under Witnessing): the register vs the board, reading the Board column and the six status chips, completion following the board, the removed-plus-completed pairing, Import Register and its no-dates guard, Match Assignees, the full Send to Board flow including the exact-change comparison, import History, and member visibility.

The YTT daily focus tutorial is rebuilt around the current feature set: the spanning three-day layout with pinned day headers, the solid status badges, the in-place expansion with the percent slider and the append-only daily updates log, and the admin constraint quick-add.

The report emails topic now covers the Morning Cx Update and its configuration, the Test vs Send now semantics on every tile, and the sent-reports archive with search, kind filters and exact-copy viewing.

Theme foundations: four variables that were used in places but never defined (bg, text, faint, card2) now have real per-theme values, plus a new linkc link colour, so the class of bug where an undefined variable silently rendered transparent or inherited is gone at the root rather than fixed per incident.

Benchmarks now follows the theme and the Display colour palette. Every status chip, the Board link chip, the Completed flag, the changed markers and the Send to Board comparison highlights colour through the shared palette variables, so High Contrast and Colour Blind palettes finally reach this page, and light mode is readable (the old chips were dark-tuned hex, near-invisible on light paper). The Send to Board dialog's bars and inputs also sat on an undefined background variable and now use the real one.

Benchmarks: a row that dropped out of the register after its witness passed keeps its Removed In ACC status (removal remains a register fact and is never masked) but now carries a green Completed flag beside it, with a tooltip noting that finished FOKs leaving the register is expected. The row stays greyed out as removed rows already were. Rows removed while still pending or failed are unchanged and keep the plain Removed chip.

Benchmarks: a new Board column shows, for every register row the matcher links to a board activity, a clickable #code chip that opens that card on the planning board; unlinked rows show a dash. The link was always computed (stored link, then FOK ref, then title match) but only surfaced on Changed rows; No Date, Completed and Removed rows now show their card too. The filter bar gains an on-the-board count, and the status chip drops its old #code suffix since the Board column now carries the code for every state.

Benchmarks completion sync: a benchmark now reads Completed when its board activity is complete or its witness is recorded as passed on the board. Derived live from the board rather than stored, so a corrected outcome (revert, retest) flows straight back.

Send to Board: changed rows carry an info icon that expands an exact comparison of what Send will change against the live card, across Title, Date and time, Assignee, Duration and ACC link, with drifted fields highlighted. The proposed side reflects the row's current edits in the dialog, so the comparison is precisely what Send will do.

Deployment skew guard: a tab opened before a deploy used to fail its next lazily loaded feature (workbook import, the icon catalogue, PDF export) with a failed-to-fetch-module error, because the deploy replaced the hashed chunk files the old tab still references. The app now detects that exact failure and reloads itself once to pick up the fresh version; a one-shot guard prevents reload loops during a genuine outage, and clears after a healthy start so repeated deploys in one sitting are also covered.

The frozen in-bundle changelog copy is deleted. It was a fossil at REV158 while changelog.json carried the truth; the app already fetched the json at load, with the fossil as a fallback whose only effect was showing months-stale entries in the weekly report if the fetch failed. The report's changelog rows now come solely from changelog.json.

Width-cap sweep: the Reports page now follows the Design > Layout content width setting (it was fixed at 1160px and ignored the variable; it keeps 1160 as its default when no width is chosen). The sweep confirmed the Activity Table, Constraints Log and Analytics already respect the cap, and the board stays full-bleed by design.

Page registry unified: one PAGE_REGISTRY const at the top of the app now feeds every page-key list, the boot deep-link whitelist, the page-restore check on refresh, and the Design system's page table (icons, naming, per-page scopes). Previously these were three separate literals and a new page had to be added to each; two of this week's integration bugs came from exactly that. The rail button and Help topic remain deliberately hand-placed, since a missing button is seen immediately, and pointer comments at those sites say where to register the page first. No visual or behavioural change.
Weekly Cx Progress no longer carries the Weekly Report builder button; the builder lives on the Reports page only.

Refreshing while on the Reports page stays on the Reports page. Two causes fixed: the page-restore whitelist read on load did not include the new page, and the admin guard could bounce even an admin to the board in the moment before project membership loaded; it now waits until members are loaded before judging.

The Reports page header is readable in light mode: it referenced a theme variable that does not exist, so a hardcoded dark fallback applied in both themes; it now uses the real surface variable and follows the theme.

Reports page modernized per the approved design: the three scheduled reports are now status tiles showing state, schedule, next send, last send and their Test and Send now actions; the daily and weekly digests present as separate tiles over the unchanged single scheduler. Next send is computed from the same boundary helpers the scheduler uses, so a tile can never disagree with what will actually happen; a disabled report reads not scheduled.

All of the Morning Cx Update's configuration moved off the page into a slide-over drawer behind Configure: enabled, send time, days, recipients, company exclusions (collapsing past eight companies) and section toggles, saving on change as before. The permanent settings form and the wall of exclusion pills are gone from the page.

On demand is two proper tiles (the Weekly Report builder and Exports), and the Reports archive gained a search box and kind filter pills (All, Morning, Daily, Weekly, Report) over the loaded rows. The superseded flat cards were removed outright rather than left as dead code.

Reports page fully integrated into the shell: the expanded sidebar now shows its label (REV248 rendered only the icon), and the page joins the Design system's page list, so its sidebar icon can be customised in Design > Icons (default: mail, greyed for other pages once used), it can be renamed in Design > Naming, and it participates in per-page design scopes like every other page.
Help updated: the Admin update emails topic is now Report emails and the Reports page, opening with where everything lives (the Reports page), the Morning Cx Update and the archive.

Hotfix: REV248 blanked the app. The Reports page guard was written as an effect whose dependency list referenced state declared later in the component, which throws before the first render can complete. The guard is now a deferred redirect placed after everything it reads exists; behaviour is identical (non-admins landing on the Reports page are returned to the board) and the crash is gone.

New Reports page, admins and the owner only, taking every report function off the other pages: the Morning Cx Update card with its configuration, the daily and weekly digest card, the Weekly Report builder and the activities export. The Analytics page keeps only its charts, gauge and drill-downs (its metrics workbook and PDF stay beside them). The nav shows Reports to admins only and the page guards direct navigation.

Reports archive: every sent report is stored and viewable. The page's Reports section lists all runs newest first with kind chips (MORNING, DAILY, WEEKLY, REPORT), status, recipients and subject, loading 30 at a time; View opens the sent email in a sandboxed viewer exactly as it went out. The full weekly report archives its light version when under 2MB, otherwise the email body. Runs sent before the archive existed show their details with a note that the content was not captured.

Fix: the report_runs kind check in the database still only allowed daily and weekly, which would have blocked every Morning Cx Update claim; the 2026-07-12_report-archive migration widens it to include morning and report and adds the archive columns. The migration must be applied for morning sends to work; archiving itself is fire-and-forget, so an unapplied migration can never break a send.

Morning Cx Update: a start-of-day email for the project team, sent via Outlook. Counts strip, finishing today with constraint status, overdue finishes (last week listed, older counted), starting today, open constraints sorted by need-by with overdue in red, yesterday's daily updates from the append-only log with author and time, and witness events for the day. House template, 560px, Outlook-Word-safe; empty sections are omitted and an empty day says so.

Fully configurable in Reports (admins): enabled toggle, send time defaulting to 08:00 Europe/Helsinki, days of the week, recipients as entire project team or admins only, per-company exclusions from the team send (for example the client), and per-section toggles. Settings live in project settings and audit as setting changes.

Scheduling rides the digest machinery: boundaries computed from the configured time and days, claimed in report_runs under kind morning so concurrent sessions cannot double-send, dispatched from the designated Outlook sender's first active session at or after the send time with the same catch-up window. Send now (with resend confirmation) and Send me a test buttons on the card; recipients resolve live at send time with anyone lacking an email named in the run detail.

Constraints can be raised directly from YTT Focus. The card expansion gains an Add constraint row (what is blocking, owner, needed-by date) above percent and daily updates, for admins and the owner, matching the YTT clearing gate. The added constraint uses the same shape as the editor's constraints, including the raised-by history stamp, appears on the card immediately, and the change lands in the audit log via the activity triggers.

YTT Focus: the Yesterday, Today and Tomorrow headers are pinned. They sit in their own block above the card grid, so they stay in view while the cards scroll beneath, per the standing pinned-header rule for list panels; the today band runs behind both the headers and the scrolling cards, and column alignment is guaranteed by both blocks sharing the same grid.

Daily updates: every YTT card now carries a chevron that expands to percent complete and an append-only daily-update log. Updates are added by admins and the owner only, each entry stamped with date, time, author and the percent at the time; entries can never be edited or removed by anyone, enforced at the database level (the new activity_updates table has no update or delete policies at all). Everyone on the project can read the log; percent editing keeps the existing rule (owning company's members, admins, owner) with the slider and number in the expansion, and the current percent shows on the collapsed card as a chip.

Every daily update also lands in the audit log as a Daily update entry (new teal UPDATE chip, filed under Activities), so Admin > Audit doubles as the project's daily-update record. Requires the 2026-07-12_activity-updates migration to be applied in the Supabase SQL Editor; until then the expansion shows a banner naming the migration instead of failing.

YTT Focus: one card per activity, spanning the days it covers. An activity running yesterday through tomorrow is now a single band across the columns instead of three duplicated cards with three constraint lists, the span itself showing where it sits in the window; a faint blue band marks today's column and small edge arrows mean the activity extends beyond the three days. Row order is global: open constraints first, then committed, then start.

Each activity now carries one status anchored to today rather than a different chip per column: FINISH OVERDUE when its planned finish has passed uncompleted, FINISH DUE with the day when it finishes today or tomorrow, STARTS with its dates, ONGOING with its end date, FINISHED for completed work. Badges moved out of the meta line to the card's top right and switched from low-contrast tints to solid fills with dark or white text, the same treatment as the WILL and WIT tags: amber for due, red for overdue, house blue for ongoing, green for starts; FINISHED stays a quiet outline so done work does not compete for attention.

Audit overhaul. The log is always visible (the Show log toggle is gone), grouped under day headers with relative times, and every entry carries a coloured category chip: added, edited, removed, done, import, export, witness, revert. One pinned block above the rows holds the search, the user filter, category pills (Activities, Imports and exports, Witnessing, Team and access, Other), the entries-created date-range view, CSV export and a live count; it stays put while the log scrolls.

The whole history is now browsable: the audit log pages from the database 100 entries at a time with First, Prev, Next and Last navigation, instead of stopping at the most recent 500. Filters and search run server-side, so page counts are true, and Export CSV exports everything matching the current filters (up to 10,000 rows), not just what is on screen. Snapshots load per page, so Undo and revert states stay correct however deep you go.

Clicking any audit row opens its detail. Activity entries with a snapshot open the diff popup directly (restores-to versus currently) with the action inside it; everything else opens a detail card with the full action, user, exact timestamp, entity and snapshot state.

Revert renamed so the risk is legible: Undo is the clean case, and its popup says this is the latest change to the activity so nothing else is affected; Force revert is the risky case, amber, with overwrites parts of N later edits under the button and the same warning inside the popup. Restore and Remove keep their names; Superseded and Reverted-by tags survive.

YTT Focus cards now separate what is ongoing from what is due, or was due, to finish. Each card's left edge and a right-aligned chip carry its execution status for that column's day: amber FINISH DUE when the planned finish is that day and it is not complete, red FINISH OVERDUE in Yesterday when the finish passed uncompleted, blue ONGOING with the end date when it just runs through the day, green STARTS on its first day, and completed cards keep the done tick with a teal edge and a faded FINISHED stamp in Yesterday. Single-day rows and milestones resolve to the due signal. Note: the card edge previously showed the Cx stage colour; in YTT it now shows execution status, and the stage colour remains on the board itself.

User Privileges: a people search and role filter now sit pinned above the matrix, in the zone that never scrolls, next to a live N of M count. Search matches names and companies case-insensitively, role pills narrow to Owner, Super, Admin or Member, section headers keep their filtered counts, empty sections disappear, and a no-match state names what you searched. Typing a company name collapses the matrix straight to that company's people.

Both import surfaces rebuilt as the approved five-step flow: Template, Mode, Upload, Review, Apply, with a progress rail that tracks where you are (template downloaded, mode chosen, file up, review open, applied). The board's Import Activities modal groups each step into its own block; the Admin Import / Export card follows the same shape, with the JSON project restore separated out under its own upload.

One rules source. The import rules are now generated per project and mode by a single function that feeds both the on-screen rules block (collapsible, next to the valid-value chips) and the workbook's README sheet, so the screen and the file can never drift apart again. Member rules spell out the company lock, including in Sync; stale claims from the pre-review era (nothing imported on any error, Sync arriving later) are gone everywhere.

Small flow touches: the user modal ends with an Import another file reset after applying, the Admin mode toggle explains what Append, Sync and Override each do as you switch, and the Admin activities upload accepts only .xlsx and .csv while .json moved to the Project restore input (both feed the same handler, so nothing changes underneath).

Sync import mode on both surfaces. Rows carrying a #code from an exported workbook update that activity: filled cells overwrite, blank cells leave fields untouched, and rows without a Code are added in the same pass. Members can only update their own company's activities; a Code belonging to another company is a rejected row. Constraint sheet rows against a synced activity append to it, stamped into constraint history.

Review before apply. Every xlsx or CSV upload now opens a review screen instead of writing immediately: each row resolves to Add, Update with per-field diffs (old value -> new value), No change, or Error with the row and column named. Filter pills pin above the grid while rows scroll. Nothing is written until Apply.

Partial apply and error report. Rows with errors no longer block the whole file: they are listed, skipped on Apply, and exportable as import-errors.csv, so a 200-row file with 3 typos lands 197 rows and a fix list instead of nothing.

The Admin importer's fingerprint duplicate warning and the Override typed confirmation now run at Apply time on the reviewed result; the old direct-commit CSV path is retired. Import results report added, updated, constraints raised, new companies and crews, and skipped error rows.

Import template: removed the Building column prefill (hundreds of repeated values added nothing the dropdown does not already do). Instead the importer now fills a blank Building automatically when the project has exactly one building; on multi-building projects a blank Building is still an error, and milestone rows keep their blank Building. Member Company prefill and the Ref column are unchanged: Company is the member lock, Ref keys predecessors and the Constraints sheet.

Import / Export v2 foundations. One import engine now sits behind both the Admin importer and the board's Import Activities, replacing two drifted copies. The new workbook template carries four sheets: Activities with every field the platform runs on (discipline, witness type, duration and days, crews, estimated hours, percent complete, predecessors), Constraints keyed by activity ref with owner and need-by (stamped into constraint history as raised by the importer), Reference feeding every dropdown from the project's live values, and README with generated rules and a version stamp that the importer checks on upload.

Member imports: the template comes with the Company column prefilled and locked to your company and the Building column prefilled when the project has one building; rows naming another company are rejected with the row and column named, never silently rewritten. Rows with no description, system and planned start are treated as blank, so prefilled columns on unused rows are harmless.

Predecessors import as semicolon lists: a plain number links to that Ref within the file, #57 links to the existing activity with that code. Rows carrying a Code are rejected for now so an edited export cannot be double-imported; Sync mode, which updates existing activities by Code, is the next revision.

Admin, Import / Export gains Activities workbook (import shape): Activities and Constraints sheets identical to the template, including each activity's Code, so export, edit and re-import round-trips. Both surfaces' descriptions rewritten to match what the importer actually does, and import results now report constraints raised and amber notices (unknown template version, defaulted values) alongside the written-row count.

Fix: opening a constraint's history on the last rows no longer forces scrolling. The card now floats over the page from the clicked row's real position, escaping the table's scroll container entirely, so it simply uses the empty space below the table; it flips above the row only when the screen genuinely runs out of room, and it closes on scroll so it never drifts away from its row.

Fix: the constraint history card now matches the approved design. It opens anchored to the constraint's own row (flipping above when the row sits low on the screen) instead of a detached corner card, and shows a proper timeline: coloured event markers joined by a rail line, hollow blue for raised, filled green for cleared, hollow amber for reopened, with the last open event marked still open. The drawer's inline history uses the same timeline.

Fix: several interface texts showed raw escape codes instead of their characters, most visibly the hub's project search box reading location-u2026 instead of an ellipsis. The crew and baseline-search placeholders, the baseline mapped-count separator, and the no-mappings hint on the schedule are corrected the same way; a full sweep confirms no other leaks remain.

Constraint audit history: every constraint now records who raised it, who cleared it, and any reopenings, with times, stamped at the moment of each action on the Constraints page, the activity drawer, and the board's make-ready and acknowledge paths. A clock button beside each constraint (Constraints page and the drawer's Readiness tab) opens the timeline. Constraints raised before this revision carry no stamps; the viewer says so and shows related audit-trail entries as best effort. Who may clear constraints is unchanged; this is visibility only.

Project Team gains Resend invites to pending: one button emails a fresh 30-day set-password link to every member who has not yet signed in, sent from your connected Outlook account, three at a time with one summary naming any failures. Members with no email on file are skipped; the button shows the pending count and asks once before sending.

Fix: percent complete on an activity can once again be amended only by members of the company that owns the activity, plus admins and the owner. A recent Record Progress change granted percent rights to any member with edit-own privileges on every activity, regardless of company; the check now requires the owning company exactly as full editing does, and the save handler enforces the same rule independently of the input's state.

Design settings tranche 5 of 5, Effects, completing the Design programme: an Effects tab with two project-wide controls. Depth (Flat, Standard, Elevated) scales the decorative shadows on card hovers, drawers, modals, and menus; Motion (Off, Reduced, Standard) scales transition speed on the rail, toggles, hovers, and expanding panels. Signal effects are exempt and always show: the search spotlight, selection rings, the online pulse, and notification badges. The app now also honours the operating system's reduce-motion preference automatically for every user, regardless of project settings. Untouched projects render exactly as before.

Design settings tranche 4, Naming: a Naming tab in Admin, Design that renames each page per project, so the sidebar and the page pickers across Design settings speak the team's own vocabulary (Lookahead instead of Planning Board, Blockers instead of Constraints Log, and so on). Reports, digest emails, invites, and Excel exports keep the standard names deliberately, so outbound documents stay consistent for clients and auditors. Admin is not renameable, empty means the standard name, and everything resets per page or all at once.

Design settings tranche 3, Board: a Board tab in Admin, Design controlling how Planning Board cards present for everyone on the project. Card edge colour by Cx level (today's default), Company (stable name-derived colour), Discipline, or Status; six card-detail toggles (commitment, witness state, constraint count, delay markers, crew hours, company/location) with hidden details still available in the drawer and table; and card text size Small, Standard, or Large. The Gantt view follows the same rules. Safety signals are not configurable: a failed witness keeps its red edge and failed chip, and a slipped activity keeps its red edge. Projects that never open the tab see no change.

Design settings tranche 2, Layout: a new Layout tab in Admin, Design with three controls, each settable globally or per page like every other design control. Content width (Narrow 1180px, Standard 1500px, Full width), Density (Comfortable or Compact, tightening row, card, and button padding to about three quarters on the working surfaces), and Corner radius (Sharp, Standard, Soft, one scale across cards, tables, buttons, and chips). Projects that never touch these keep today's layout exactly; print output and emails are unaffected.

Fix: the hub comparison headers now sit exactly over their columns. The final column sized itself to its own content in each grid, near zero in the header row and about 110px in data rows, so the header's other columns spread wider and drifted right; the final column is now a fixed width, giving the header and every row identical geometry. This asymmetry predated the target column and became visible with it.

Fix: the hub Project comparison columns are aligned again. REV223's target subline could widen its column differently in each row because every row lays out independently; column widths are now locked so content can never stretch a track, headers and rows share identical geometry, and long sublines truncate instead of pushing.

The hub's Project comparison table now judges PPC against each project's own Delivery-settings target: green at or above target, amber within 10 points below, red further out, grey until a project has eligible commitments, with the target and signed gap shown under each figure. The portfolio Excel export gains Target and Gap columns. Targets are still edited inside each project; the hub reads them.

Design settings now carries the full lucide icon library: press Browse the full icon library on the Icons tab to search 1,700+ icons and set any of them as a page's sidebar mark, with the same duplicate guard as the built-in set. The library downloads as a separate on-demand chunk, so projects that never open it pay nothing; the main app bundle is unchanged.

Send All Pending on the Witness Schedule now sends three invitations in parallel instead of one at a time, cutting bulk send time to roughly a third on busy session days. Each event's create, logo attach, and attendee steps stay strictly ordered, results are summarised in one message with any failures named, and retest confirmations are asked once up front instead of per activity.

Microsoft Graph throttling (429) is now absorbed automatically: sends wait out the advised delay and retry up to twice before surfacing an error, protecting single sends, bulk sends, report emails, and digests alike.
The Activity Table and the Constraints page now sit inside the same 1500px centred column as Reports and the other pages, instead of stretching edge to edge on ultra-wide monitors.

Multi-project digests: the daily and weekly admin update claims are now taken per project, so FIN04 and future projects can each send their own digests on the same date without blocking one another. Digest subjects, titles, and footers carry the project's own name and client line, and the Open DLP button deep-links straight into the sending project.

Project-scoped audit trail: every audit event is now stamped with its project at the database level, and the in-project audit views and digest content read only that project's events plus platform-level ones. Existing history is attributed to FIN04.
The printed Weekly Report masthead now shows the project's real location from the project record instead of a hardcoded one.

Domain-move groundwork: every email link, sign-in instruction, and report footer now uses the address the app is actually running on rather than a hardcoded dlp-pi.vercel.app, and browser preferences migrated from the legacy fin04_ prefix to dlp_ with an automatic one-time carry-over.

Project branding threaded through outputs: witness invite subjects, user invitation emails, weekly report emails, the printed report masthead and title, and every export filename now take the project name from Settings branding; hub-level invitations are project-neutral (plain DLP).

Project Team self-heals company associations: each time the member list loads, every member's employer is linked to the project, covering bulk adds, access request approvals, employer changes, and historical gaps in one idempotent sweep. Orphaned code from the retired project-level Users tab removed.

Global Vendors arrives (Phase 4): Global Settings gains the platform vendor directory with the pinned search block, name, category, notes and a Used-on column showing which projects map equipment to each vendor. Add rejects duplicate names in any casing; Manage renames, categorises, annotates and removes directory entries, always with the ground rule stated in the UI: project equipment mappings store plain text and keep what they were saved with.

Each project's Vendors tab suggestions now combine the global directory with locally used names, and assigning a vendor name the directory does not know adds it there automatically, so the directory grows itself from real project work.

The picker sweep (Companies model, final phase): every place a company is chosen now offers only the companies on this project. That covers the activity editor's Company (Performing) select, inline edits in the Activity Table, the company filters on the Activity Table, Project Team and report drill-downs, constraint and risk owner pickers, new-activity and import defaults, the CSV template's company list, and the per-company breakdowns in Analytics and the Weekly Report.

Name resolution stays global everywhere, so historical rows and other-project references never display blank; selects editing an existing row also include its current company even if it has left the project, so nothing snaps to empty.

Anything that starts referencing a company now associates it automatically: creating or matching a company inline from the board, and adding a person to the project, both register the company on the project. If association data is unavailable the pickers fall back to the full registry rather than going blank; a genuinely empty project shows an empty picker until companies are added on the Companies tab.

The digest failure toast for an expired Microsoft session now shows one instruction instead of two welded together: when the error is a sign-in matter the toast reads a single clear line pointing at Settings > Outlook & SharePoint, replacing the underlying error text rather than stacking guidance on top of it. Non-auth errors are unchanged. The toast continues until Outlook is actually reconnected, because sending is genuinely blocked until then.

A house scrollbar standard, applied everywhere in both the project app and the hub: thin bars with a transparent track and a subtle rounded thumb that brightens on hover, replacing the heavy OS-default bars that stood out on Vendors, Systems, Locations, Companies, Project Team, User Privileges and the portal. The Project Team scroller stays (it is what keeps the filter bar pinned and the presence panel independent) but is now a hairline.
The Global Settings sub-nav (Directory / Platform) floats: it stays in view while the page scrolls.

Project Team and User Privileges now fill the viewport exactly: the roster column and the presence panel each scroll themselves on Project Team, the privileges table absorbs all remaining height with its own scrollers, and the settings body no longer grows a wrapping scrollbar around content that already manages its own scrolling. On narrow screens the team layout reverts to natural stacked flow.

The Changelog arrives in its new home: Global Settings gains a Platform section with a Changelog viewer that reads the deployed changelog live. Every revision is collapsible (newest expanded, with a LATEST tag and change count); the pinned search matches revision numbers, dates and change text, expands matching revisions and highlights the hits.

Settings pages are now viewport-fitted: the settings body scrolls internally and the page itself never grows a redundant outer scrollbar. On short pages like Design and Access requests there is no scrollbar at all; on long ones a single inner scrollbar does the work, alongside the tables' own scrollers on User Privileges.
Companies and Vendors obey the pinned-header rule: the Companies column headers, and the Vendors filter row plus its table headers, stay in view while their lists scroll.

The digest failure toast no longer stacks two messages into one garbled sentence: the underlying error is shown once, and when it is a Microsoft sign-in matter the toast points at Settings > Outlook & SharePoint and states that retry is automatic on the next tick after reconnecting.

The Changelog tab leaves project settings (it had been frozen at REV102) ahead of its new home in the hub's Global Settings. The Weekly Report's changelog rows now read the deployed changelog.json fetched at session start, so they are always current without a per-revision source edit.

The project Companies tab now shows only the companies on this project, drawn from the project_companies associations, instead of the entire global registry. Two ways to add: pick an existing company from the global registry, or create a new one, which registers it globally and adds it here in one step; a name that already exists globally is added rather than duplicated.

Delete company on the project tab is replaced by Remove from this project: it severs the association only, is blocked with exact counts while any activity (including soft-deleted) or team member on this project references the company, and never touches the global registry or other projects. True deletion lives only in Global Settings.

Name and Role edits in the project modal remain, with a note that renaming is shared globally. Pickers and filters elsewhere in the app still read the full registry; scoping them to this project's companies is the next revision.

Global Companies gets the same pinned block as Global Contacts: a search field (name or domain, with a live match count) and the column headers stay in view while the registry scrolls. This pinned filter-and-header block is now the standing default for every list panel in the platform.

Company logos move to their global home: the Manage modal in Global Settings now carries the light and dark logo slots (upload on the correct swatch, remove with one click; changes reach every project). The project Companies tab no longer uploads logos and instead points to Global Settings. The registry list shows per-slot L and D status chips.

Fixed the stray horizontal scrollbar inside hub modals: the project app's box-sizing reset is scoped to a root class the hub does not have, so hub inputs overflowed their containers by their padding. The hub now carries its own scoped reset.

Global Companies arrives in Global Settings: the platform-wide company registry is now managed from the hub. The table shows every company with its email domain, logo status, a CLIENT tag where the name matches a project's client, and a Used on column listing the project codes it is associated with (from the new project_companies table).
Add company creates a registry entry with an optional email domain; a name that already exists is rejected rather than duplicated.

Each company's Manage modal renames it (with the same duplicate guard; the new name shows everywhere the company is referenced), edits its email domain, and offers Delete from registry. Deletion is blocked with exact counts while any activity or person anywhere on the platform references the company; an unreferenced company deletes after confirmation, sweeping its project associations away with it. Logo uploads stay on each project's Companies tab.

One page, one scrollbar: the portal shell is now a fixed-viewport layout like the project app, so the body never scrolls and the redundant outer scrollbar is gone. A single full-width scroll region sits beneath the fixed chrome and above the fixed footer; the Global Contacts panel loses its own inner scrollbar, and the pinned filter, export and column-header block holds at the top of that one scroll region while the list moves beneath it.

Global Settings: the filter bar, the Export Users button and the column headers now stay in view together while the contact list scrolls. The contacts panel becomes its own scroll area (the same pattern that keeps the filter bar frozen inside a project), with a single pinned block at its top; page-level pinning is abandoned after failing in the browser regardless of what the stylesheet declared. A side effect worth having: the Directory sub-nav also stays in view, since the page itself no longer scrolls.

Global Settings correction: the filter bar (Search, Company, Platform, Invite) now freezes beneath the fixed chrome while the contact list scrolls, matching how the filter bar behaves inside a project. It was already sticky but pinned at the top of the viewport, hidden behind the fixed bars; it now holds at the correct offset and spans the panel edge to edge. The column headers scroll normally again, reverting the REV205 reading of the request.

Portal chrome pass: the top bar and the tab bar are now fixed on every hub page (Home, Projects, New project, Inside a project, Analytics, Global Settings), and the DLP by QMC footer is fixed to the bottom of the viewport, right-aligned in the same style as inside a project. All dropdowns and modals stack above the fixed chrome.

The hub now remembers where you were: refreshing while on Global Settings (or any other portal page) returns you to that page and, within Global Settings, to the same sub-tab, instead of dropping back to Home. Pages that need platform-admin rights fall back to Home for anyone without them.
Within Global Settings, the directory column headers (Person, Email, Company and so on) hold in place beneath the chrome while the contact list scrolls.

Hotfix for the hub Global Contacts rendering unstyled: the scoped stylesheet shipped with REV203 carried an unclosed rule (the multi-line .lk-btn rule was truncated by a line-based extraction), which silently invalidated every rule after it, leaving filters, the roster grid, group headers and inputs at browser defaults. The stylesheet is rebuilt with a brace-aware extraction, and delivery checks now include a structural validity gate: balanced braces per rule and across the sheet.
The hub directory now renders identically to the former project tab: styled filters, collapsed company groups with chevrons, the email-column roster grid, and the proper inputs, buttons and modals.

Global Contacts moves to the hub: a new Global Settings tab on the portal bar (owner and super only) carries the platform-wide directory with the same filters, per-company grouping, email column, Export Users, Add person, Bulk Add and Manage modal as before. Global Companies and Global Vendors appear alongside as placeholders for the next phases.

Global Contacts leaves project settings. Project admins manage their people entirely from Project Team: adding a person there already creates the platform contact, and Resend set-password link and Reset password now show their card on the Project Team tab itself instead of jumping to the removed page. The Edit name, company or platform role dialog is unchanged and now reports its result inline.
Editing a contact from the hub takes effect for every project at once, which is exactly why it now lives above them rather than inside one.

The User Privileges matrix is now grouped by company: Platform team pinned first (owner, then supers), each company alphabetically with its people sorted by name, No company last. Company bands span the matrix with the label held in the sticky user column, so it stays visible while scrolling across the privilege groups.
A contractor's whole block can now be reviewed at a glance. Toggle behaviour, amber deviation dots, Reset To Role Defaults, the group filter and the save bar are all unchanged.

Project Team gains Add A Person To This Project and Bulk Add Users To This Project: both create the platform account when the person is new and add them to this project in the same step, as the chosen project role. Existing Global Contacts (matched by email) are added to the project without a new account, and people already on the project are reported rather than duplicated.

Bulk results tell the truth per row: Created and added, Added (existing), Already on project, or the exact failure, with the set-password links CSV and Email All Created Invites carried over from Global Contacts. A failed project grant after a successful account creation is reported on the credentials card instead of being swallowed.

The Project Team footer no longer sends you to Global Contacts to create accounts; project admins can now build their team in one place. The project role select grants project-level admin only; platform roles are untouched.

Project Team gains an email column, a project-scoped Export Users (CSV) button under the member count, and a per-company grouped roster that starts collapsed, with Platform team pinned first and No company last, exactly like Global Contacts. Searching auto-expands the groups; search now also matches email addresses on both tabs.
Global Contacts gains the same email column on every row, and its search matches email addresses as the placeholder always promised.
The team export file is named from the live project code rather than a hardcoded FIN04, with columns Name, Email, Company, Project role, Projects, Status and Last seen.

The AI NARRATIVE badge is removed from Weekly Report narrative blocks. The narratives themselves, their italic left-border styling and the methodology note are unchanged; the blocks now read as part of the report rather than flagging their origin.

Show Completed now actually shows completed. The past filter (Showing Present & Future) was silently overruling it: completed work is past-dated by nature, so every completed row failed the past filter no matter what the toggle said, and the switch read as dead. Completed rows are now exempt from the past filter entirely; their visibility is governed by the Show Completed toggle alone, while the past filter keeps suppressing stale pending work exactly as before.

The Register change log was blind to most of what a register actually changes week to week: its stored snapshots kept only Title, Planned date, Assignee and Discipline per ref, so edits to Notes or ACC links between imports honestly reported as no differences. Snapshots now carry ACC link and Notes as well, the diff compares all six fields, long values truncate in the display, and comparisons against imports taken before today judge only the original four fields rather than inventing phantom changes.

A benchmark whose board activity is complete now reads Completed, wins over No Date, sorts to the bottom, appears in the status filter, and is hidden by the Show Completed toggle exactly like a manually archived row. Previously board-finished FOKs sat as On Board forever, so with past dates filtered out the toggle appeared to change almost nothing.

Import hardening, defensive only, no behaviour change on healthy files: the planned date column header now has a substring fallback if it is ever reworded, the import result states how many planned dates it found, a file carrying zero planned dates where the previous import carried many is interrupted for explicit confirmation before overwriting, and the change log flags any date-blind import it is asked to compare.

The Risk Register on the latest imported week now counts overdue from today instead of the pack's declared reporting week, because risks are live obligations and a stale pack must not make the register look calmer than reality; today that difference was 4 overdue shown against 6 real. Historical weeks stay frozen to their reporting week, every view now states its anchor (as of today, or as of week ending 26 Jun), and an amber chip appears on the latest week whenever the imported pack declares a week more than 7 days old, matching the existing ACC staleness signal. The insights bar signal and the row popups follow the card's anchor so no two numbers on one screen disagree. Render-time only: every previously imported snapshot corrects itself, no re-import needed.

Every Cx pack import, uploaded or synced from SharePoint, now asks the importer to declare the reporting week before anything is written. The field pre-fills from the workbook's detected week cell, the declared value wins and is what the snapshot is filed and anchored under, and amber notes flag a declaration that differs from the workbook or a week that ended more than 7 days ago. This closes the gap where a pack whose week cell was never rolled silently re-imported as an old week.

Hotfix: Generate report died silently after REV194. The narrative helper was declared partway through the report builder while the Schedule Snapshot, which uses it, is assembled earlier, so building the report hit the constant before its initialization and the exception killed the click without a trace. The helper now sits at the top of the builder, verified by executing the extracted builder end to end in a harness rather than by compile checks alone, which is how this one slipped through.

The Schedule Snapshot in the weekly report now shows its link logic. Every activity whose start is pushed by a late predecessor carries an amber tag naming the days and the driver (pushed +5d by #12), computed by the same forward pass the board's forecast chips use, and the section opens with a short narrative naming the chains: which predecessor drives the movement, what it pushes, and how many activities hold their planned dates.

AI narratives across the report, not just the executive summary: Schedule Snapshot, Plan Reliability, Status Mix and By Contractor can each carry a short model-written paragraph under the section header, switched per section in Configure with an optional per-section steer and a report-wide tone line, all saved with the report settings. The model only ever sees a block of drafted facts, its output is rejected if any figure, date or name drifts from those facts, and when AI is off or unavailable the drafted facts themselves render as the narrative, so a printed report never blanks and never says more than the data does.

The exec summary Polish flow is unchanged. Requires the updated super-action edge function (section mode, additive, polish path untouched); until it is deployed, narratives render their deterministic fallbacks.

Match Assignees on the Benchmarks page now resolves the way a person would: the local part of an assignee email is treated as a name (jukka.mechelin matches Jukka Mechelin in Global Contacts), which resolves both the person and their company; explicit company domains still apply; and every person match teaches the run that domain's company, so an unfamiliar address at a known domain resolves in the same pass. The result message now says what matched by person, what by domain, and names any domains it could not place, with the two ways to fix them.
