The Assignee column shows the resolved person's name where one exists, with the raw email on hover, instead of the bare address.

The Benchmarks and Register change log titles now carry the same heading colour as every other page, and the change log states exactly what it compares (Title, Planned date, Assignee, Discipline per FOK ref), so a no-differences verdict on a re-saved but content-identical workbook reads as the evidence it is.

Override imports are now gated behind an explicit confirmation: a modal states exactly what will be wiped and rebuilt, how many activities will be replaced by how many incoming rows, and requires the project code to be typed, case-sensitive, before the button enables. Append mode never sees the gate.

Every import (CSV, xlsx and JSON) is content-fingerprinted per project. Importing a file whose parsed content matches a previous import is interrupted with when, by whom, in which mode and how many rows, before any duplicates are created; the check is stored in the database (new import-safety.sql migration, run before the app files) so it holds across users and browsers, and renaming the file does not defeat it. Fingerprints prune to the newest 50 per project automatically.

The global company directory can no longer be damaged by an import or a bulk save: imports only ever add companies (Override no longer resets the vendor list, and JSON imports remap incoming vendors onto existing ones by name so activity links hold), and the database sync now refuses any state diff that would delete more than one company at a time, keeping the deliberate single-company delete via Manage intact. This closes the root cause of the 10 July incident.

Realtime is now scoped to the project you have open. Previously any change by anyone in any project reloaded every connected client; with two live projects that meant FIN04 and Vantaa users constantly reloading each other. Now a change on a project-scoped table only reloads clients viewing that project, while shared directories (companies, people, memberships, privileges, branding) still refresh everyone.

Reload churn removed at the source: presence heartbeats, digest run claims, audit rows and revert snapshots no longer trigger reloads at all. Each either rides alongside a primary data change that already fires its own scoped event, or (presence) has always been polled on its own 30 second cycle. Deletes and unrecognised payloads still reload conservatively, so nothing can be missed, only skipped when provably irrelevant.

Witness invite repair no longer dies when the calendar event is missing. If Realign and Update or Update Invite tries to update an event that has been deleted or was created under a different Outlook account, DLP now creates a fresh event for that day and swaps it in, rather than failing the whole action with an object-not-found error. The result line says plainly when a day was re-sent because its previous event no longer existed. Cancel is tolerant the same way: an event that is already gone is treated as cancelled instead of erroring.

Behind Plan witness rows now say where the plan went. A rescheduled invite that is still anchored to its old date shows an amber line naming the new planned date and the date the invite is still on, so it is clear the reschedule was picked up and Realign and Update will move the witness session onto the new date and send the correction. Closes the gap where a Behind Plan card looked as though nothing had changed.

Witness invites now follow the plan. Rescheduling an activity, whether from the Schedule tab or by dragging it on the board, moves its witness session by the same number of days and keeps the time of day, so the invite no longer stays silently anchored to the old date. The row on the Witness Schedule flips to Details Changed and Update Invite lights up; press it to send the correction to attendees. Nothing is emailed automatically on a reschedule or a drag, the explicit Update Invite click still governs every send.

Invites that were left behind by a reschedule made before this change are now caught rather than sitting as Sent. Any sent invite whose witness date has drifted outside the current plan is flagged Behind Plan with a one-click Realign and Update that snaps the witness time onto the new start (time of day kept) and sends the update in the same pass.
Corrected the Witness Schedule connect hint, which previously claimed reschedules sent updates automatically. It now describes the real behaviour.

Crew Capacity is now a per-project setting, off by default and enabled only where crew-hour planning is in use (FIN3021 Vantaa is switched on by the migration; every other project starts clean). The toggle lives at the top of Settings, Lookahead and Targets, admin only.

With the toggle off, every crew surface disappears on that project: the Crew chips and Est hours field in the activity editor, the Crews tab under Project Setup, the Capacity pill on the board, the hours chips on activity cards, and the Working Week and Working Hours per Day controls that exist only to feed capacity. Turning it off hides, never deletes: saved crew and estimated-hours data is kept and returns intact if the toggle comes back on.

The importer is deliberately unchanged: Crew and Est hours columns in a file still parse and store silently on any project, so an import cannot fail over a column the project does not display. Needs the crews-toggle.sql migration before data.js and App.jsx.

Completion window safeguard: a member can mark an activity Complete up to and including its planned finish day; from the next day, Complete is admin-only. The drawer greys the button with an amber explanation directing the member to record the Reason for Non-Completion and ask an admin; admins see a blue notice that they are recording an overdue completion, and on an overdue completion the Actual finish no longer auto-fills with today, so the true date is set deliberately.

Every path is bound, not just the drawer: the Activity Table inline editor, bulk set-status and find-and-replace all apply the same rule, and the bulk and find-and-replace result messages state exactly how many rows were skipped as past their planned finish. A new database trigger (completion-window.sql, run before the app files) refuses non-admin completions past the window regardless of client, surfacing through the red Database error banner if anything routes around the UI.

Completion audit made explicit: transitions to Complete now log as Marked complete with the planned finish, actual finish and an on-time or overdue verdict (naming the admin when recorded after the window); setting a complete activity back logs as Reopened activity. Field-level diffs and revertible snapshots continue underneath unchanged.

New Capacity pill on the board toolbar: a crews-by-days grid of estimated hours across the board window, coloured against the project working calendar (green under 85% of the working day, amber 85 to 100%, red over). Non-working days are greyed, and hours planned onto one are flagged with a dashed red outline. Each crew row totals its hours for the window; every cell explains itself on hover.
Activity cards now carry an hours chip when Est hours is set, showing the hours and the assigned crews at a glance.

Load rules, stated plainly: multi-day activities spread their estimated hours evenly across their planned days; joint activities load every listed crew with the full hours; completed activities drop out of the load. Working week and hours per day come from Settings, Lookahead and Targets.

Database errors are no longer silent. If loading a project fails on any table, or a save is rejected by the database, a red Database error banner now appears at the top of the app naming the table and the exact error. Previously a failed load rendered an empty project and a failed save was quietly reverted by the live refresh, both with no message anywhere.
The import result message now reports what was actually built (activities and new crews), not just how many rows were parsed, and states that a rejected save will surface in the banner.
Diagnostic revision: no behaviour changes beyond error visibility and the import message.

Crew is now a first-class, per-project field. Manage the crew list in Settings, Project Setup, Crews (Owner and Admin), and assign an activity to one or more crews in the editor Details tab. Renaming or deleting a crew updates every activity that used it. This replaces holding crew in free text, which was fragile to load against.

New Est hours field on the editor Schedule tab: the hours each listed crew spends on the activity. It is the input the crew Capacity view will read; nothing depends on it yet this revision, so existing activities are unaffected.

Per-project working calendar in Settings, Lookahead and Targets: choose the working week (global default Mon to Fri) and the working hours per day (default 8). These set the denominator and the greyed days for crew capacity, and stay scoped to the project you are in.
Import now recognises Crew, Est hours and Discipline columns and auto-creates any new crew, so a whole L4 programme can be seeded with its crews and hours in a single import (append mode).
Groundwork only: the Capacity view itself lands next. Run supabase/crew-capacity.sql in the SQL editor before pushing data.js and App.jsx.

Board reschedule fixes. A rescheduled activity now earns its own lane row: the packer reserves the reschedule origin span (the dotted trail and the origin ghost), not just the new start, so a neighbour can no longer be packed on top of the trail. Failed-invite and retest chains are unaffected: the change applies only to activities carrying a reschedule, and the retest row preference still keys on the current start.

The origin ghost of a rescheduled witness invite now reads as the superseded invite rather than an empty dashed box: the activity code struck through, and a re-send flag when the original invite had been sent, so it is clear the moved event still needs re-issuing. Nothing in the data or the sent Outlook invite changes; re-sending stays a deliberate action from the editor.

Colour and Typography are now per-page as well as global. Each has an Applying to selector: pick Global default to set the project-wide look, or pick a page to override just that page's accent, heading font or body font. A page shows a dot when it differs from the default, overridden controls are labelled, and a page can be reset back to inheriting the global value. Global stays the base that every page inherits.

Applied consistently: the theme helper takes an optional page key and merges the global override then the page override, so the per-page look reaches the own-root pages (Table, Schedule, Admin) as well as the inline and external pages via the shared content wrapper. No new column; the pages block lives in the existing design setting reserved for it in REV180.
This completes the Colour and Typography categories (global plus per-page). Remaining Design categories still to come: Layout, Board, Effects and Naming.

Design section gains Colour and Typography, alongside Icons. Colour sets a project-wide accent (preset swatches or a custom colour) used across buttons, links and highlights. Typography sets the project heading and body font from the fonts already loaded (Space Grotesk, Inter, System, Serif, Mono), with a live preview. Both have a reset to default.

These apply everywhere consistently: the theme helper now carries the overrides so they reach every page and every modal, drawer, Table, Schedule and Admin view that renders its own theme root, not just the main content. Stored in a new design column; run supabase/settings-design.sql before pushing data.js and App.jsx.

Scope note: Colour and Typography are project-wide in this tranche. Per-page overrides for them are next; they need page context threaded into the few components that render their own theme root, and the global path should be confirmed on screen first. Icons remain per-page as before.

Fix: per-page sidebar icons chosen in Settings, Design did not persist and reverted to default almost immediately. The choice was held in memory but the settings writer had no field for it, so the realtime settings reload rebuilt the row without it. Added a page_icons column and mapped it on both read and write, so the icon now sticks. Run supabase/settings-page-icons.sql before pushing data.js.

New Design section in Settings (Admin, under Appearance), first tranche: a per-page sidebar icon picker. Each page picks its own icon, and any icon already used by another page is greyed out so no two pages can share a mark. This resolves the two identical ticks: Weekly Cx Progress keeps the tick and Benchmarks now defaults to a shield, and either can be changed. Choices are stored as a project-level default and the sidebar renders from them, falling back to the built-in defaults.

The built-in icon set grew from 33 to 54 (target, layers, clipboard, database, flag, bell, sliders, folder, truck, award, hexagon, compass, gauge, hammer, users and more), all in the same line-icon family so nothing looks out of place.

Scope note: this is the Design home plus the icon system. The deeper theming (colour, typography, density, board and effects, with the global-default-plus-per-page-override model) follows in its own tranches, because each needs CSS-variable plumbing across the app and should not ship in one unverified lump. Adding the full external icon library is a separate follow-up so it can be lazy-loaded rather than bloating the main bundle.

New Register change log on the Benchmarks page (History button, Owner and Admin). Its primary view compares one import against an earlier one: it lists benchmarks added since, benchmarks removed, and benchmarks changed with the exact field that moved (title, planned date, assignee or discipline) shown old-struck-through to new. Two selectors let you compare any two imports, defaulting to the latest against the one before, and a summary counts added, removed, changed and unchanged.

Each import now writes an immutable snapshot of the register (ref, title, planned date, assignee, discipline) to a new append-only table, so the history cannot be altered after the fact and every import is auditable. The first import after this ships has nothing earlier to compare against; comparison begins from the second. Run supabase/acc-benchmark-imports.sql before pushing data.js and Benchmarks.jsx.

The board Case control (Orig, CAPS, Title) has moved off the toolbar and up into the top bar next to the theme toggle. It was widening the toolbar enough to wrap, which pushed Export and the Activity button onto a second row; the toolbar is now back to a single row. The control renders only on the board and only for Owners and Admins, so members no longer see it and their board shows original-case names.

Members can now record Percent complete on any activity, including another company's, so a trade can report progress on work that is not tagged to their own company. Only percent is opened: status, actual dates, witness outcome, reasons and the whole plan stay read-only for other companies' activities, and setting percent to 100 does not mark the activity Complete because status is unchanged. The activity editor shows Record Progress with a Save progress button for these cases.

How it is kept safe: the activities update policy stays company scoped, so nothing else opens up. A single SECURITY DEFINER function, set_activity_percent, is the only route by which a member reaches another company's activity, and it can change nothing but percent. The change is audited to the member who made it. Run supabase/member-percent.sql before pushing data.js and App.jsx.
Own-company and admin editing is unchanged and still goes through the normal save path.

New Case control on the board toolbar (Orig, CAPS, Title): sets how activity names render on the planning board. It is display only and remembered with your other board preferences; the stored text is never changed, so search, exports, witness invites and the audit log are unaffected. Title case preserves acronyms and codes: any word that is already all caps (EPODS, UPS, PLC, MV), mixed case (iDCM) or contains a digit (RET615, GY03/04) is left as written, minor words (and, for, of, x as a multiplier) are lowered, and ordinary words are capitalised. A name typed entirely in capitals is left as it is, since an all-caps word cannot be told apart from an acronym without a dictionary.

Fix: the Display palette flyout was clipped off the left of the screen when the sidebar was expanded, because it was a fixed width pinned to the rail's right edge. It now pops out to the right of the rail in both the collapsed and expanded states, so it is always fully visible.

On-board lookup is now robust and visible. A benchmark is matched to the planning board by its stored link, then its FOK ref, then an exact normalised title match, so a FOK already on the board without a ref is still detected. The On Board and Changed In ACC pills now show the matched activity code (for example On Board #42, hover for detail) so you can verify and find it on the board.

Send to Board uses the same three way match, so it updates an existing board activity instead of creating a duplicate even when the match is by title rather than ref. Purely a matching and display change; no schema or push order change beyond the three files.

Benchmarks page reworked (cumulative with REV170 and REV171). CSA now stays on the benchmark board and never goes to the planning board: Send to Board only offers disciplines that have witness routing, which excludes CSA. New Match Assignees button resolves each assignee to a company (an email by domain, a name against the user directory) and stores it, shown in a new Company column and used as the activity company when sent. Buttons now use the planning board lk-btn styles.

New Hide Past toggle zooms the board to the present and future; benchmarks can be marked Complete to drop out of the default view and Restored from a Show Completed filter. Sticky header no longer bleeds (z-index fix). New activities default their area to L0 & L1 - Site General. Run supabase/acc-benchmarks-fields.sql to add company_id, resolved_email and completed_at.
Sending still never commits or emails: activities are created uncommitted with invites Not Sent (or none where the per row Invite toggle is off), released later through Send All Pending.

Send to Board is now a confirm and fix step. The modal shows the assignee clearly and lets you correct it, the title and the date, and add or confirm the time and duration per row, with defaults applied to all rows unless overridden. A per row Invite toggle controls whether a witness invite is created: it defaults on for disciplines that have routing and off for CSA, so CSA benchmarks are added to the board without an invite as requested.

Sending never commits and never emails. Activities are created uncommitted with invites in the Not Sent state, or with no invite where the toggle is off. You commit them in the lookahead, then release invites through Send All Pending. The confirmation message states the uncommitted, unsent state explicitly. Cumulative with REV170 (the Send to Board feature itself), so pushing these files applies both.

Send to Board on the Benchmarks page (Owner and Admin). It opens a modal of the dated benchmarks that are ready or changed, with a default witness time and duration plus per row overrides, an attendees preview from the discipline routing, and per row selection. Confirming creates board activities, or updates the existing one for a changed benchmark, as L2 FOK witness activities with invites in the Not Sent state, then links the benchmark. Nothing is emailed; invites are released as usual through Send All Pending.

Faithful to the mockup: only dated, sendable benchmarks are listed; the time and duration defaults apply to every row unless a row overrides them; changed benchmarks are relabelled and update in place rather than duplicate; the footer count and the Send button track the selection. Creation runs through the same update path and toActivity mapping the board already uses, with witnessType L2 FOK and the register discipline routed via witnessContacts.
Note: witnessContacts has no TO list for CSA, so CSA benchmarks currently route to CC and organiser only until a CSA discipline list is added. Electrical and Mechanical route to their full lists.

Fix: importing or syncing the Weekly Cx Progress workbook no longer overwrites an existing week silently. The reporting week is keyed on the date the workbook itself reports, so re-importing a file whose Reporting week ending cell was not rolled would replace a prior week's snapshot in place. If the target week already exists and the incoming data differs, a Confirm overwrite dialog now lists the fields that changed (stored vs incoming) and requires an explicit choice. Cancelling explains that a new week needs the Reporting week ending cell rolled in the source file first. Identical re-imports still proceed without a prompt.

Note: this guard does not advance the reporting week on its own. If the source workbook is still dated to an older week, the correct fix remains rolling the Reporting week ending cell and refreshing the ACC extract at source. The Open issues by type card and the Risk overdue figures are driven by that same source data (the Calc sheet and the reporting week respectively), so they refresh once the source is current.

Fix: the FOK import failed with "ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE command cannot affect row a second time" when the register reused a FOK Register ID (ref 8160 appears twice on the CSA sheet). writeBenchmarks now dedupes by ref before the upsert, last wins, and the import message reports how many duplicates were collapsed so a data-entry dup in the register is visible rather than silent.

New Benchmarks page. It reads the acc_benchmarks staging table and shows each FOK register row against the board with five statuses (ready, changed, on board, no date, removed in ACC), filterable by discipline and status. Import Register (Owner and Admin) loads the real multi sheet FOK workbook through the tested import engine and upserts it, so it is the manual path to use while ACC authorisation is pending.

Member visibility is admin controlled. The page is Admin and Owner only by default; a Visible to members toggle at the top, itself Owner and Admin only, opens it to all project members, persisted per project (run supabase/benchmarks-visible.sql to add settings.benchmarks_visible). Both the rail button and the route enforce the gate.

Send to Board is deliberately held for the next revision so the activity creation and witness routing are built against the live invite path rather than rushed, since that is the one step that touches real invitations.

Manual FOK register import engine (src/benchmarkImport.js), the path to use while ACC authorisation is pending. It reads the real multi sheet workbook: walks the discipline tabs (Electrical, Mechanical, CSA), skips the overview and list sheets, locates the header wherever it sits, pulls the ACC link off the title cell hyperlink and formats dates. It produces the same benchmark rows the ACC webhook will, so both feed one staging table. exceljs loads on demand to keep the page light.

The parser in accReconcile.js was reworked against the actual FIN04 register: it locates the header row rather than assuming row one, tags discipline from the sheet, handles the shifted CSA layout, and keeps emails lowercased while leaving name assignees as they are. Proven on the real file, 173 benchmarks parsed across the three disciplines with hyperlinks and dates correct.

New writeBenchmarks in data.js upserts an import into acc_benchmarks, marking any ref that dropped out as not present while preserving board links. Dormant until the next revision wires the Benchmarks page Import button, the table and Send to Board.

Benchmarks foundation. New project scoped acc_benchmarks staging table (run supabase/acc-benchmarks.sql). The ACC sync upserts the parsed FOK register into it and never touches the board; the Benchmarks page reads it and Send to Board is the only bridge from a benchmark to a real activity.

Status derivation added to accReconcile.js (benchmarkStatus, benchmarksWithStatus) plus a loadBenchmarks reader in data.js, all dormant this revision. Thirteen harness assertions cover the five states: removed when a ref drops out of ACC, no_date, ready when dated but not yet on the board, changed when the board date or assignee has moved, and on_board, plus the activity join and the summary counts.

Next: the Benchmarks page and the Send to Board modal wired to the existing FOK activity creation and witness routing, then the acc-webhook receiver, delivered as a self contained single file so it deploys cleanly from the dashboard, upserting into this table.

ACC webhook security core added (supabase/functions/_shared/accAuth.js): the auth and verification the live receiver depends on. Web Crypto only, so it runs identically in the Supabase edge runtime and in the test harness, and holds no secrets.

Three functions, fully harnessed across fifteen assertions. Inbound Autodesk webhooks are verified by HMAC-SHA1 over the raw body against the x-adsk-signature header, so tampered bodies and wrong secrets are rejected. dm.version.added events are matched to the watched FOK file by item URN or name hint, so unrelated activity in the folder is ignored. An unattended token is minted as the Secure Service Account robot via an RS256 signed JWT assertion, whose signature is verified against the public key in the harness.

This is the credential independent security layer of the webhook receiver. The handler that composes it with the fetch (acc.js) and reconcile (accReconcile.js) engines, and the live token exchange, activate once BIMMS authorises the robot and the real folder, item and secret values exist.

ACC reconcile engine added (src/accReconcile.js): the logic that turns the FOK register into board changes. It is pure and dormant this revision, makes no ACC call and is not yet wired in. A header driven parser (tolerant of column renames and reorders, the same lesson as the asset register) feeds a fok_ref keyed diff that produces adds, changes and removed candidates using canonical change signatures.

Two guardrails are built in and tested: invites are only ever produced as pending, so releasing them stays a human action through Send All Pending, and a row dropping out of the register is a removed candidate, never an auto delete. Fourteen harness assertions cover header order tolerance, idempotent re-runs, witness versus non witness changes and the no send and no delete guarantees.

This is the credential independent brain of REV163. The activation layer (Secure Service Account auth, the acc-webhook receiver, the pg_cron reconcile and the board Sync Now button) is held until BIMMS authorises the app on their ACC account, because those need the robot key, the real folder and item URNs and the webhook secret, none of which exist yet.

ACC Live Sync groundwork (one way, ACC to DLP, Owner and Admin only). New project scoped tables acc_sync and acc_sync_events (run supabase/acc-sync.sql in the SQL editor) hold the watch config, status and event log. Project scoped from the start so a second project cannot collide the way report_runs does.

New Connections card for Autodesk Construction Cloud sits beside Outlook and SharePoint in Settings > Connections. It reads acc_sync and shows Not Configured until the ACC service identity is authorised. The live watch, reconcile and the board Sync Now button follow in REV163 once the unattended token model is proven.

New src/acc.js carries the Data Management crawl and signed S3 download, region aware (x-ads-region EMEA for Koski), returning the same buffer shape as the SharePoint reader so the FOK parser and invite pipeline are reused unchanged. Harnessed against a stub; no live ACC call is made in this revision.
New edge function acc-admin, Owner and Admin verified server side, exposes the status op now; every mutation returns a not yet enabled response until REV163.

Hotfix: the Readiness drawer went blank for any witness activity with a discipline. REV160 defined the assignee-aware recipient resolver inside the App component only, but the Drawer is a separate component, so its recipient preview hit an undefined reference and unmounted the page. The resolver is now a module-level function, in scope for both App and Drawer. No data or invite behaviour changes; recipients resolve identically.

FOK invites now carry the assignee. A new activity field, assignee_email, adds the column H contact to the required attendees and shows an Assignee row in the invite body. Recipients are resolved through one shared helper so the change hash, the send, and the editor preview always agree. Activities without an assignee are unchanged, so no existing invite flips to Details Changed.

Seeded a second FOK batch for FIN04 Koski from the FOK Register V2: nine Electrical (Velox, assignee Antonio Rodrigues) and one FLS sprinkler item M-702 (FireCon, assignee Jukka Mechelin), each L2 FOK at 10:00 to 12:00 with the ACC link and register id. Titles cleaned of the Forms Build suffix.

The seed first renumbers the earlier M-114 from register id 8303 to 8368 to match the V2 register, freeing 8303 for the new Electrical earthing FOK and removing the id clash between the two register versions.

New admin view: Settings > Audit > View Entries Created lists activities by the date they were entered into the system (their database insert time), not their planned date. Presets for This Week (Sunday to now on your browser clock), Last Week, This Month, plus a custom from and to range. Each row carries the same detail as the analytics drill popup, with one line added: who created it and their company. A summary strip counts entries per creator and there is a CSV export

Attribution note: activities inserted directly by SQL that did not record a creator (for example the original bulk import) show as Unattributed; their insert date is still accurate. Deleted activities do not appear (the view reads live rows only). Admin and owner only

Status Mix card on the Analytics page filled out. The donut is larger, each status band now carries a full-width composition bar of its internal split (ready versus make-ready, on time versus finished late), and a health strip beneath adds three rollups the card did not surface before: readiness (share of planned work with no open constraints), on-time completion rate, and the make-ready backlog. Every element including the three tiles drills to its activities. In progress keeps its flag counts rather than a split bar, since running late and constrained can overlap.

Executive summary in the weekly report now carries the same numbered section heading as every other section; it becomes section 01 and the rest renumber automatically, with the KPI tiles remaining beneath it as the at a glance numbers.

AI polish can now be steered. A short optional instruction beside Polish with AI (for example more concise, lead with the MV risk, or formal board tone) is passed to the polish so you can shape tone, length, emphasis and order. The existing guard is unchanged: any figure, date or name not already in the draft is rejected, so a steer can reshape the summary but never invent a number. Steering takes effect once the updated super-action edge function is deployed; without it, polish still works and simply ignores the steer.

Weekly report visual fixes to match the app. Status mix: a very thin band (for example In progress when it is only a handful of activities) now keeps a minimum visible width and drops its inline label instead of clipping it, so the colour always shows and the exact count reads from the card below.

Committed next week, Milestones, and the Schedule snapshot now colour by Cx stage, matching the planning board. The next-week card edge and the milestone diamond take the tag colour (readiness state stays on the pill), and the schedule snapshot bars and legend follow the Cx stage rather than status.
Cx attainment KPI grid filled: Blue tag (L4) and White tag (L5) tiles added so the eight tiles complete the grid and the empty grey space is gone; null counts read n/a.

Risk register in the report now matches the app: Responsible, Raised and Overdue columns, overdue rows sorted first by days overdue with the year inferred from the reporting week, overdue cells flagged red, and priority shown as a coloured tag.

Committed next week rebuilt as a decision snapshot. Each upcoming committed activity is now a card with a coloured left edge for its state (ready, make-ready or at risk) and signal chips that appear only when they apply: witness type and time, the open constraint count with the nearest need-by date, predecessor codes with any incomplete one flagged, a forecast push in days when a predecessor knock-on moves the start, and the reschedule history. Notes render in full. A summary line tallies how many of the week's commitments are ready, make-ready and at risk.
The forecast push reuses the same non-destructive forward pass as the planning board, so an incomplete or slipped predecessor moves a successor consistently across the board and the report.

Weekly DLP Report brought in line with the Analytics page. By contractor and By Cx stage are no longer plain bar charts of headcount: each row now carries a segmented volume bar (complete, in progress, planned), a reliability figure (PPC for contractors, share complete for Cx stages) and its open constraint count, so a reader sees who is delivering and what is blocked in one line. Both read programme to date, matching Analytics with filters at All, and each states its scope.

New Status mix section in the report: the whole programme split into planned, in progress and complete, with each band broken into its actionable sub-states (ready to run versus needs make-ready, running late versus constrained, on time versus finished late). It is the print form of the Analytics Status Mix and is on by default.

The Percent Plan Complete hero is now the semicircular gauge used on Analytics, with the target tick and colour bands (green at or above target, amber within 15 points, red beyond), replacing the plain number.

The weekly PPC trend is upgraded from a sparkline to the Analytics grade chart: a dashed target line, a 4 week rolling average, the current in progress week drawn hollow with a dashed lead in, and the kept of due figures printed under each week since paper has no hover. It now covers 8 weeks so the average has room.

New Witness reconciliation under Plan reliability: every witnessed test failure across the programme is placed into on-time-failed, late-failed, not-yet-due or not-committed, always summing to the total.

Fixed the palette popout layout. A global sidebar rule that sizes rail icon buttons to 40px was also catching the popout option buttons, forcing them narrow so the labels spilled outside the box. The popout styles now take precedence: labels sit inside the box with proper padding on the left, and the colour swatches line up in a column on the right.

Stopped the palette popout bleeding onto the sidebar rail: widened the gap between the rail and the popout, and gave the popout shadow a contained rightward spread so its soft edge no longer washes back over the rail.
Tidied the palette popout: widened it so no option label wraps, gave every row the same height, kept labels aligned on the left and the three colour swatches aligned in a column on the right.
Palette menu now opens clear to the right of the sidebar rail with a small gap, instead of overlapping it, and the colour swatches sit on the right of each option so they no longer crowd the rail.

The Display palette now reaches the whole app, not just the data pages. The planning board delay bars, the activity table, constraints, the schedule, admin status pills and the sidebar PPC figure all follow the selected palette. Status colours are now theme and palette aware: Default is unchanged, High contrast and Colourblind safe apply everywhere.

Email, weekly report and PDF colours are deliberately left untouched, since those must carry real colour values that mail clients and the PDF engine can render. The embedded Help content and the subtle translucent delay tints on the board are the remaining surfaces and will follow in a later revision.

Palette menu tidy: the selected option is now a neat pill on the label itself, not a full-width block, and the colour swatches sit cleanly inside each row. The selected highlight no longer looks misplaced.
Display control moved to sit directly above the PPC figure at the foot of the rail, as one bottom cluster, instead of being stranded under the last nav icon.

New palette glyph for the Display control, replacing the half-circle that read too much like a light or dark toggle. The palette menu swatches now show a clean three-dot status preview (received, warning, outstanding) for each option.

New Display control at the foot of the left rail, directly above the PPC figure, available to every user: pick a colour palette (Default, High contrast, or Colourblind safe). The choice is personal, saved per user in this browser next to your theme and view preferences, and applies immediately across the app.

High contrast deepens the status colours and lifts text and gridline contrast on the data-dense pages (Documentation Tracker, Asset Status, Weekly Cx Progress). Colourblind safe remaps status to an Okabe-Ito friendly set: received and on-track read blue, outstanding and overdue read vermillion, so telling received from outstanding no longer relies on a red versus green pair.

Status colours now follow the palette across the whole app, including the planning board (late, forecast and on-track), the three data pages and the PPC readout. The semantic commissioning colours (tag stages L1 Red through L5 White and the physical tag bands) are deliberately left unchanged, since those encode the tag level, not a status.
The project portal (the project-picker screen) keeps its current colours for now and will follow in a later revision.

Vendor Status now counts VEN/CON documents, not just VEN. A shared vendor/contractor document (for example the Vendor/Installer Pre-Start Up Checklist) now contributes to a vendor's received-of-applicable figure, so L2 Yellow Tag shows figures for vendors it previously left blank. The same rule now applies to the per-row vendor figure on the main matrix; its note reads VEN and VEN/CON columns.

Vendor Status, an info button beside each vendor name lists exactly which documents are outstanding, grouped by tag level, with the equipment types affected. A vendor with everything received reads All vendor documents received. Close with the X, Escape, or a click outside.

Documentation Tracker, Vendor Status window (admin and owner only). A new Vendor Status button opens a table of vendor owned (VEN) documents grouped by vendor and tag level: received of applicable per level, with a due date you can set per vendor and level and a live delay chip (Complete, Due soon, Due today, Overdue by N days, or outstanding with no due date). Unassigned equipment types are grouped under Vendor TBC. Requires the SQL migration supabase/docs-vendor-target-REV144.sql to be run once in the Supabase SQL editor; until then the window opens empty and the page still works.

Documentation Tracker, click a column header to read its full title. Clicking a header (in Codes or Labels mode) opens a popover with the full document name, its tag level, the responsible party, and how many equipment types have received it. The column highlights while it is open; close with the X, Escape, or a click outside. The angled labels were hard to read at a glance.

Digest claim resilience. A transient transport failure on the report_runs claim (the browser's Failed to fetch, a cold or briefly unreachable database) no longer aborts the whole digest with a red toast. Transport errors retry a few times with a short backoff and, if still unreachable, show a soft note and retry on the next tick. A duplicate claim or an RLS block is unchanged, so stuck-claim adoption still works. This does not fix a genuinely unreachable database; frequent occurrences are environmental.

Toolbar continuity. Sync From SharePoint is now the purple button on the Documentation Tracker to match Asset Status, and Edit Mode is neutral when off and fills blue when active with its label switching to Editing.

Weekly Cx Progress can now sync the Cx Master straight from SharePoint. A new Sync From SharePoint button sits beside Import workbook and opens a window that shows the tenant connection, an editable file URL, and Run Sync. It pulls the workbook bytes and runs the exact same parse and week upsert that a manual upload runs, so there is one parser and no new table. The file URL is remembered per project in the existing cx_config.

The sync window includes a Use the Asset Status URL button that copies the URL already configured on Asset Status, since it is the same physical file. Note: the URL is held as a separate per project copy on this page, so if the file ever moves it must be updated in both places until a single shared project level pointer is added.

AssetStatus pager moved and restyled. The Prev, range, Next control has left the top action bar and now sits on the filters row, right aligned, as a compact bordered pill. The range shows in accent bold with of N muted, and Prev and Next are chevron icon buttons that fill accent blue on hover. Paging behaviour is unchanged: the pager only appears when the filtered list is longer than 200 and filtering resets to the first page.

Digest emails no longer print raw JSON. Setup-table audit rows (settings, profiles, companies, areas, systems, levels) that stored the row as JSON are now humanised at render time: settings show as a lookahead line, profiles as name and role, and the others as short labels. Any unrecognised JSON degrades to a plain verb plus entity, never the blob.
Digest classifier now routes profile changes to the Users bucket and other setup changes to the Project admin bucket, instead of dumping them all into Other.

audit_setup database trigger rewritten to store a human-readable detail per table rather than left(row_to_json(row), 400), so newly written audit rows are legible at source. Existing rows are still cleaned by the render-time humaniser.

Documentation Tracker header redesigned. Each angled column label now sits in its own header cell, sharing the exact column the data dots use, so the label and its column are always the same width and a long label can never sit over a neighbouring column. Column width tracks the chosen angle: steeper angles give narrower columns. Angle choices are now 90, 75, 60, and 45 degrees.

New Codes header mode. A toggle on the filter row switches between full angled Labels and compact horizontal Codes (short four character codes, with the full name on hover and in Reference). Hovering a column links it to its header label.

The Overall sign off column was removed from the tracker, along with its Signed Off tile and the signed and not signed row filters. The sign off value is still read and stored from the Master, so nothing is lost and it can be brought back later.
The per row vendor figure now counts vendor owned (VEN) document columns only, so it reflects what the vendor is actually responsible for rather than every column.

FOK witness invites. The witness invitation now carries the external FOK Register ID as its own detail row and a LINK TO ACC FILES button that opens the Autodesk ACC field report, placed above Open This Activity In DLP. Both render only when the activity supplies them, so every other invite is unchanged.

Two new activity fields, acc_url and fok_ref, added to the activities table and mapped through the data layer so they load, save, and survive edits. The ACC link also feeds the invite change hash, so editing it re-sends the meeting update.

Seeded eight future dated First of Kind sessions for FIN04 Koski from the FOK register (three Electrical, one Mechanical, four FLS), each set to L2 FOK, 10:00 to 12:00 Europe/Helsinki, one session, with the ACC link and register id attached.

New Documentation Tracker page. An equipment type by document matrix driven by the Cx Master (sheet Documentation status), synced from SharePoint by the same mechanism as Asset Status. Five commissioning tag levels (L1 Red through L5 White), tri state cells (received, outstanding, not applicable), per column responsible party, a manual overall sign off flag, and a live received against applicable percentage.

The column headers can be angled at 90, 60, or 45 degrees from a toggle on the filter row, and every label stays fully visible at any angle. Filter by level, responsible party, vendor, and status; group by lead level or vendor; and click a column to focus it.

Admins can edit the matrix in Edit Mode: click a cell to cycle received, outstanding, not applicable, and click the Overall column to toggle sign off. Manual edits survive the next sync; where a manual value clashes with the incoming file, the sync summary lets you keep yours or take the Master.

New Vendors section under Settings, Project Setup. Admins map each equipment type to its vendor or OEM; the mapping shows on the Documentation Tracker as a chip and drives the vendor filter. A new Edit Documentation Status privilege gates the tracker Edit Mode.
Excel export of the filtered documentation matrix.
Asset Status now loads with every type collapsed, so you see the whole list at a glance and open only what you need. Changing the Group By dimension re-collapses to the new grouping.

Real bulk editing in Edit Mode. Each type header has a checkbox that selects the whole type in one click, even while it is collapsed, so the flow is collapse all, tick the types you want, then apply. Inside an expanded type, click one row then Shift-click another to select the range between them, the same behaviour as the activity table. The selection bar gains Select all, which takes the whole filtered list, alongside Deselect all, and the type header shows an indeterminate state and a running count when a type is partly selected.

Asset Status now renders at most 200 assets per page, with a Prev and Next pager at the top right showing your position in the filtered list. Even Expand All never builds more than 200 rows, which is the main lever for keeping the page fast on the full register. The KPIs, tag pipeline and Excel export still cover the whole filtered set; only the matrix is paged. Changing a filter returns you to the first page; editing a cell leaves you where you are.

Asset Status is faster, with no change to how it looks or works. Two things made the page heavy: every status edit refetched the whole 1096 asset register from the server before redrawing, and the search box redrew the entire matrix on every keystroke. Edits now update the changed cell in place and only resync from the server if the save actually fails, so setting a status is immediate instead of a couple of seconds. The search defers the heavy redraw so typing stays responsive. Behaviour and appearance are identical; only the speed changes.

Fixed: the RFFE email and subject now lead with the asset code, matching the PDF, instead of the friendly name. The code (for example FIN04.01.L0.LB01.AC101) is bold and primary; the name (for example Air Curtain) sits in grey parentheses as context. Velox SAP identify assets by code, and a type name like Air Curtain is not unique, so the code belongs up front. The EE completion line references the code, and the single asset PDF filename uses the code too.

The Open This Asset In DLP link in the RFFE email now lands where it should: it opens the Asset Status page and, for a single asset send, opens that asset's overview drawer (checkpoint status by stage, planned versus actual, overall progress). A combined send opens the Asset Status page. The link is captured before boot rewrites the URL, so it survives the redirect, and the URL is tidied to the project afterward.

Closing the loop: when the EE column is marked complete (by the Velox SAP team, who cannot send email themselves), DLP now sends an automatic confirmation to the admins, Karolina, Ruain, Damian and Mark, that the asset or assets are Equipment Energised. It is a green house-style email, code primary, with a link straight to the asset overview. It is sent from the designated DLP session and deduplicated by a one-time claim, so it goes exactly once even with several tabs open; if several assets are energised close together they are combined into one email. This needs the small REV136 SQL migration applied first.

Fixed the automatic daily and weekly digest emails, which never sent while you were logged in. The scheduler timer was wired to the digest function through a closure captured at mount, but that function is declared below the app's initial loading guard, so on the first render it was never defined and every scheduled tick threw silently instead of sending. The manual Send Now buttons were unaffected, which is why those worked. The timer now reaches the digest through a ref kept current on every render, so the daily 17:00 and weekly Friday 16:00 sends fire, with the 72 hour catch-up. This is the reason it was worth fixing before reusing the pattern; the REV136 energisation confirmation runs off a different, self-refreshing poll and never shared the fault.

Fixed: your Outlook connection is now recognised everywhere from the moment the app loads. Before, the app only noticed your delegated session once you opened the Witness Schedule, so the RFFE window thought you were signed out and offered Connect Outlook even though Settings showed you connected. The connection is hydrated at boot now, so Send RFFE is live straight away without reconnecting.

Fixed: the energisation badge on the envelope climbs on its own. It refreshes the instant you set a Yellow Tag in Edit Mode, on a timer, and whenever the tab regains focus, rather than only when you open the drawer.

Added: Test To Me in the RFFE window. It sends the exact same email and PDF to your own address only, marked [TEST], with no Velox or Cc recipients and no change to the asset state, so you can check it in your own inbox before the real send.

Energisation notifications. When an asset is Yellow Tagged, whether by hand in Edit Mode or by a SharePoint sync, DLP now raises a persisted notification to admins. The bell badge counts them, and a new Ready For Energisation section sits at the top of the notification drawer, one card per asset, showing the tag and the time Cx approved it.

Request For Energisation in two clicks. Each card has an Email Velox SAP (RFFE) button that opens a compose window with everything preset to the agreed rules: the four Velox SAP addresses plus the sap.vantaa group mailbox in To, the CSN and CTS people always in Cc, and the house style email carrying the asset list and a link back into DLP. You review and send from your own Outlook; nothing sends by itself. When several assets are ready, tick combine and send them as a single RFFE.

The Registration Form For Energisation is generated as a PDF and attached automatically, filled with the fixed details and the asset tags as the location. Download it on its own first if you want to check it. SJA and supporting documents stay outside DLP as agreed.

Built to protect the initial load. jsPDF is code split so it only downloads when you actually compose an RFFE, and the Microsoft sign in library stays lazy loaded exactly as before. Validated with a render harness for the compose window and a unit test proving the PDF generates.

Asset Status Edit Mode. Admins get an Edit Mode toggle that makes the status matrix editable: click any cell to set it, or tick assets and bulk set them, for example bulk Yellow Tag. Every manual change is stored as an override, held separately from the register, so it shows a small marker with a who and when tooltip and, most importantly, survives the next SharePoint sync instead of being wiped by it.

The Velox SAP team get a tightly scoped lane. On a Yellow Tagged asset, and only there, they can mark EE complete; they have no broad Edit Mode and cannot touch any other cell. The database enforces this through row level security, so the limit holds even if the interface is bypassed.

Sync now asks before it overwrites your work. When an import would change a cell you set by hand, the sync summary lists exactly those clashes and lets you keep yours or take the import, per row or in one click for all. Non-conflicting changes still apply automatically as before, so a normal sync is unchanged.
Shipped with a jsdom render harness that mounts the matrix in Edit Mode and asserts it renders, the safeguard that was missing when an earlier edit mode collapsed the grid in production.

Asset energisation, backend and email layer. Shipped first, ahead of the UI revisions, so nothing user-facing changes yet and the live Asset Status page is untouched. Two new tables sit outside the register: asset_override stores manual status edits separately, so a SharePoint re-import never wipes a hand-set value, and asset_event is the persisted ready-for-energisation feed, keyed uniquely per asset so the Yellow Tag event fires once and survives re-imports.

A new Assets privilege group adds Edit Asset Status, on for admins, and Mark Equipment Energised, the narrow Velox lane that is off by default and granted per user. It is enforced in the database, not just the interface: row level security allows an EE edit only on an asset that already holds Yellow Tag and only by a granted user or an admin, while every other status cell needs the broad Edit Asset Status capability. The four Velox SAP members are granted EE on FIN04 by the migration.

Database triggers raise the ready-for-energisation event on the Yellow Tag transition from both the manual and the import path, flip it to energised the moment EE is marked complete, and retract rather than delete it if a Yellow Tag is later cleared, so an RFFE that already went out always leaves a record.

The RFFE request-for-energisation email gains a builder in the house Outlook-safe template that matches the witness invite: the blue FIN04 header bar, the asset or assets in bold with muted tags, a bulleted list for a combined multi-asset send, an Open In DLP button, the 48 hour reminder callout and a provenance footer.

Fix: the gauge tutorial sliders (PPC and target in Reading the gauge) could be moved by clicking at a point but would not respond to a held drag. The simulator rebuilt its whole panel on every input, destroying the slider you were dragging. It now updates only the gauge, the number labels and the sentence on input, leaving the sliders in place, so a held drag slides smoothly

Fix: the Help page showed the old flat Quick Reference / How To navigation instead of the new chapter tree, and the new tutorials were unreachable. The visible Help nav is rendered by the app itself (the help file's own nav is deliberately hidden when embedded), so the earlier help rebuild updated a nav that never showed. The app's Help nav is now the eight-chapter tree (Getting started, The board, Planning and delivery, Witnessing, Reporting, Assets, Administration, Tutorials) with a working search, matching the help content exactly so every page and every Try It tutorial is reachable

All nine newer reference pages (Quality-Adjusted PPC and targets, Failed witnesses and retests, Asset Status matrix, Weekly Cx Progress, Projects and the hub, Roles and privileges, Connecting Outlook and SharePoint, Admin update emails, Managing people) and the three interactive Try It simulators (Reading the gauge, The reconciliation line, Asset Status) now appear in the Help navigation and open correctly
The help file's own search box is now hidden when embedded, since the app supplies the navigation and search

Fix (infrastructure): the in-app Help could keep showing an older version after a help update was deployed, because the browser and CDN cached the static help file. A vercel.json now sets Cache-Control no-cache, must-revalidate on help.html, so the browser and CDN revalidate it on every load and always pick up a newly deployed help file. Unchanged help still returns a fast 304, so there is no speed cost. This fixes the caching at the HTTP layer and no longer depends on bumping a version token by hand
One-time note after deploying this: hard-reload the Help page once (Ctrl+Shift+R) to clear the copy already cached in the browser; from then on it stays current automatically

The board window (how far ahead the Planning Board draws) is now selectable by everyone from the board toolbar, not just admins. It is a per-user view preference stored in your browser beside your view, grain and lane choices, so your window is yours: changing it never moves the horizon for anyone else and never writes to the project. Two people can run the same project at one week and eight weeks at the same time. A new user opens at the project's default window

The Lookahead Length control has been removed from Admin > Lookahead & Targets; the window is no longer a project setting. The stored project default is retained purely to seed each user's first visit, and PPC Target and the Make-Ready Window stay on that page

Help gains three interactive Try It simulators, completing the REV126 rebuild: Reading the gauge (drag PPC and the target, watch the colour bands and the marker), The reconciliation line (toggle witness failures between buckets, watch the line recompute and the Quality-Adjusted PPC figure move only for on-time kept promises), and the Asset Status matrix (click a stage card to filter by tag or a column header to focus a checkpoint). All three reuse the real gauge and reconciliation builders

Five reference pages rewritten to full depth for what shipped since REV80: Analytics (the semicircular gauge, Quality-Adjusted PPC, the reconciliation line, the genuine Paretos and Status Mix sub-splits), Admin (Connections, Lookahead and Targets, the privileges matrix, hub project management), the Planning Board (Hide Complete, the failed-witness and retest behaviour), Sign in and scope (the multi-project hub, roles), and Markers and chips (the failure tail, retest chips, PASSED and FAILED, invite type and the deep link)

Fix: the in-app Help iframe served a stale cached help.html across deploys, so a help update could appear not to land until a hard refresh. The iframe now carries a version token (HELP_VERSION) bumped whenever the help file changes, so browsers always fetch the current help

Help rebuilt (Help REV 10), the first content update since REV80. The navigation is now a collapsing chapter tree (Getting Started, The Board, Planning and Delivery, Witnessing, Reporting, Assets, Administration, Tutorials) instead of a flat wall of pages; only the active chapter opens by default, and a mandatory full-text search box above the tree filters every page by title and body text, showing matches with a snippet and jumping to the page

The Analytics PPC illustration in the help is now the semicircular gauge with the target tick, matching the app; the retired full ring is gone from every help surface including the interactive analytics simulator

Two documentation bugs fixed. The witness pages no longer teach the retired CSV-export-plus-Outlook-macro-plus-Mark-Sent flow (removed from the reference page, the Analytics page and the witness and weekly tutorials); they now describe Connect Outlook, Send All Pending, per-row Sent status, Invite Type, the itemised constraints, the deep link and Test To Me. The PPC definition is corrected to the due-based REV106 meaning (only promises that have come due), replacing the stale all-committed wording

Nine new help pages at full depth, each with a faithful illustration: Quality-Adjusted PPC and targets (gauge, target, reconciliation line), Failed witnesses and retests, Asset Status matrix, Weekly Cx Progress, Projects and the hub, Roles and privileges, Connecting Outlook and SharePoint, Admin update emails, and Managing people

Groundwork for REV127: new gauge and reconciliation illustration builders are in place. REV127 will add interactive Try It simulators for the gauge and target, the reconciliation line and Asset Status, and rewrite the older thin reference pages (Analytics, Admin, Board, Sign-in, Markers) to full depth

The Analytics page is now centred on the screen and capped at the same 1500px width as the Weekly Cx Progress page, so the two pages line up when you switch between them instead of Analytics hugging the left edge on a wide monitor

The Analytics filter bar (Company, Building, Cx Stage, Period and the report buttons) no longer bleeds wider than the content beneath it; its left and right edges now sit flush with the KPI cards. It stays sticky with its background and border. The Weekly Report PDF output is unchanged, since the print styles already override both rules

The Analytics PPC display is now a semicircular gauge instead of the full ring: a half-circle sweep on a fixed left-to-right scale with the red target tick crossing the arc and its numeric label outside it, reading as an instrument against a target rather than a pie. The value, PPC label and TARGET caption stack in the open lower half; hovering the tick names the target; clicking the gauge still drills to the committed-due list

Everything around it is untouched: the target-relative colour rule (green at or above, amber within 15 points below, red beyond), the Quality-Adjusted PPC figure, the witness-failure reconciliation line, the stretched KPI cards, the sidebar figure, the weekly report and the Excel export

New witness-failure reconciliation line under Percent Plan Complete on Analytics: when any witness failures are in the filtered scope, one line states the total and splits it into clickable buckets that always sum to it: on-time kept promises (the quality gap that Quality-Adjusted PPC subtracts), late (already broken promises inside PPC's misses, not subtracted twice), not yet due (a promise finishing today is due tomorrow), and not committed (outside PPC entirely). Each count drills to its exact activities, zero buckets are omitted, and the line follows the page filters. This makes the board's ghost count reconcile against the gauge on screen instead of in your head; the calculation itself was verified correct against an independent SQL recomputation of live data

Fix: the blank strip below the KPI cards in the Plan Reliability row is gone. The row's height is set by the taller gauge card, and the card grid previously top-aligned and stopped; the eight cards now stretch to share the full column height with equal rows. Scoped to that row only; print output and every other card grid are unchanged

Fix: per-project admins could not reset passwords, resend set-password links or see live team statuses; the admin-users edge function only accepted legacy profile admins, supers and the owner as callers, so project admins got Admins Only from every menu. The function now admits per-project admins with strict scoping: they reach only people who share a project where they are admin, never the owner, never a super, and cannot delete platform accounts. Apply the three anchored edits in admin-users-REV122-patch.txt to the LIVE function source and deploy BEFORE pushing the app files; never paste the stale repo copy

Hardening shipped with the same patch: a super account can now only be managed by a super or the owner (previously any legacy admin could reset a super password through auth.admin, which database triggers never see), and only supers can change platform roles through the update op

The Project Team manage window now carries Reset Password and Resend Set-Password Link directly, beside the existing edit and remove actions; the credential card appears on Global Contacts and the window takes you there

Fix: Connect SharePoint could permanently fail in a tab with interaction_in_progress after an abandoned or failed sign-in redirect (for example picking a cs-nordics account against the Quantum MC single-tenant registration and landing on a Microsoft error page). Both Connect SharePoint and Connect Outlook now detect the stale sign-in flag, clear it and retry once; a SharePoint sign-in return is also processed at boot rather than waiting for the Asset Status page to open, so the account lands and the flag clears wherever the redirect returns. Immediate workaround before this ships: close the stuck tab and connect in a fresh one

New Admin > Connections tab (Outlook & SharePoint): both integrations as status cards showing the connected account or Not Connected, with Connect / Reconnect running the same redirect flows as the Witness Schedule, report window and Asset Status sync window, which all keep their buttons

Asset Status layout rebuilt on the single-scroller pattern (the REV108 cure): the page leaves the outer page scroller, the title, KPI, tag pipeline, filter and focus rows are fixed, and the matrix is the one scroll region, keeping its sticky band headers, column headers and pinned asset column; the hardcoded viewport-height guess that caused the double scrollbar is deleted. The footer stays pinned by the shell

New Quality-Adjusted PPC beside the Analytics gauge: the same calculation as PPC except an on-time completion whose witness outcome is Failed does not count as kept. It answers the client question (of the work promised, how much was done on time AND passed) without touching PPC itself, which remains the orthodox Last Planner reliability metric everywhere. The figure is clickable and drills to the exact on-time witness failures; it hides when the filtered set has none

New info icon beside Percent Plan Complete on Analytics and beside PPC on the expanded sidebar: opens a popup spelling out how PPC, Quality-Adjusted PPC and the target are calculated, with a worked example. The collapsed sidebar carries the same explanation in its hover tooltip

New per-project PPC Target, set by admins in Admin > Lookahead & Targets (the Lookahead tab, renamed; FIN04 seeded at 80%). It draws as a red marker and TARGET caption on the Analytics gauge, a dashed red line on the Weekly PPC Trend, and a TARGET chip with met / missed on the weekly report hero. Run settings-ppc-target-REV121.sql before pushing data.js and App.jsx

The sidebar PPC colour now keys off the target instead of the old hardcoded bands: green at or above target, amber within 15 points below, red beyond. At the FIN04 target of 80 the only visible change is the amber floor moving from 50 to 65. The expanded sidebar also shows a small amber QA figure when it differs from PPC

Weekly report: the Plan Reliability hero gains the quality-adjusted figure (only when the week contains on-time witness failures, otherwise the hero is unchanged), the target chip, a gap sentence naming the failures, and a one-line method note. The auto-drafted executive summary states PPC against the target and adds the quality-adjusted line when relevant. The Analytics Excel metrics sheet carries PPC target and Quality-adjusted PPC rows

Deliberately untouched: every base PPC predicate (sidebar, editor miss check, weekly report, Analytics, portfolio hub, digest), so no historical week restates. The digest email and help pages are follow-ups

Witness invitation emails now carry an Open This Activity In DLP button linking straight to the activity: the link opens DLP, enters the right project and opens that activity's editor. Recipients without a DLP account land on the sign-in screen; project members (including client viewers) go straight through after signing in

The link is built from the address the invite was sent from, so it follows the app.qmc.cx domain migration automatically with nothing hardcoded; invites sent before the migration keep working as long as the old address remains a redirect
Opening a deep link to an activity that has since been deleted lands on the board without an error

Witness invite emails now itemise the open constraints instead of stating a bare count: each open constraint is listed in the amber block with its text, owner and need-by date, sorted overdue first then soonest need-by, capped at six rows with a '+ N more, full list in DLP' overflow line. A need-by date before the session date renders in red with an OVERDUE tag; overdue is judged against each session day's date, not the moment of sending. Activities with no open constraints omit the block as before

New mandatory Invite Type on the Readiness tab when Witness invite is toggled (L2 FOK, W2, IVC, L3 FOK, L3 SU, L3 SAT, L4 FPT, L5 IST): it appears in the invite subject (FIN04 - L3 SAT INVITE FOR ...) and as an Invite Type row in the invite body, is copied onto retests, plan-locks with the other witness fields, joins the audit revert comparison, and shows as a pill on Witness Schedule cards. Existing witnessed activities are not disturbed on deploy: the type joins the invite change-detection hash only once set, so already-sent invitations do not flip to Details Changed until you actually assign a type
Needs the witness-type-REV119.sql migration run before data.js and App.jsx

Fix: the SharePoint sync failed with a Graph 400 (This API is not supported for AAD accounts) because the workbook read was chained off the /shares path, which the Excel workload rejects for work accounts. The sync now resolves the pasted file URL to its drive and item identity through the shares endpoint, then reads the register through /drives, the addressing the workbook API documents. No configuration change needed; the pasted URL, tenant setup, and connection all stand

Fix: the SharePoint sync modal claimed a connection using the Outlook (cs-nordics) account, which can never read the Quantum MC tenant. MSAL shares its account cache across sign-ins on the same origin, so the SharePoint module now hard-filters accounts by the file's tenant; the modal only shows connected when a Quantum MC account is present, and the two sign-ins (Outlook on cs-nordics, SharePoint on quantum-mc) live side by side without touching each other

Fix: Sync Settings was unreachable before the first import because the button lived in the toolbar, which only renders once a register is loaded. It now sits in the always-visible page header next to Sync From SharePoint, and the sync window itself shows the configured target file and worksheet, or a red warning with an Open Sync Settings shortcut when the URL is missing

Cx Step Reference upgraded to the full Test Steps Legend: step number, pre-requisite step, description, purpose, OFCI/CFCI, executed by, signed off by, and notes, seeded for all 20 legend steps including FWT. FWT is legend-only: it appears in the reference popup with a legend-only badge but never as a matrix column, since the register carries no FWT data. Admin editing moved to a labelled grid editor under the clicked row

Fix: the Asset Status sidebar button rendered with no icon because REV115 referenced an icon key that did not exist in the icon set. A package glyph (equipment asset box, feather style matching the existing set) now joins the icon map and the button points at it

New Asset Status page: an equipment commissioning matrix built from the Asset Cx Register. 1096 assets render under coloured L1-L5 stage bands with per-step status dots (complete, outstanding, not applicable), collapsible groups (by Type, Hall, Level, or Discipline) with per-stage rollup bars, sticky headers and identity column, per-asset progress, search, discipline / hall / level / type / status filters, Hide Complete, density-safe layout, and an Excel export of the current view

Tag pipeline and step focus: clicking a stage card at the top reduces the matrix to assets holding that tag; clicking any step header opens a focus view defaulting to Outstanding with Complete / All one click away and honest counts (complete, outstanding, not applicable) in a banner. Both stack with all other filters

Cx Step Reference: the info button beside the step headers opens a reference matrix (step, stage, definition, executed by, signed off by). Admins edit the content in place; register imports refresh the structure but never touch admin-authored text

SharePoint sync (admin): Sync From SharePoint reads the Asset Cx Register directly from the live Cx Master in the Quantum MC tenant through a dedicated app registration (PKCE, Files.Read.All, no client secret), addressed by the file's pasted browser URL via the Graph shares endpoint, so no folder path is ever configured. The workbook upload path remains as the fallback and both routes share one parser

The register parser maps columns by header name under the workbook's own stage bands, never by position: between W25 and W26 the register dropped SFAT and W3 and renamed FAT/DOF, QC and CYT to FAT, W1 and IVC, which would have silently corrupted a positional import. Every import and sync reports rows read, added, updated, and unchanged, plus any skipped columns

Overdue vs plan surfaced throughout: assets past a planned tag date without the tag get a red ring on the tag cell, an OVERDUE chip in the drawer's Planned vs Actual table, and a clickable KPI tile filtering the matrix to them
Platform hub: the header and the Home / Projects / Inside A Project navigation bar are now frozen, staying in view however far you scroll

Inside A Project is now a live snapshot of every project you can see, one collapsible section per project with its accent band, activity, completion and overdue counts, and an Open Planning Board button. One toolbar (Swimlane or Gantt, Day or Week, grouping, span and paging) drives all sections at once; collapse states are remembered, the most recently visited project loads first, and beyond eight projects collapsed sections load only when expanded

New Edit Project on the Projects page (owner and platform supers): pencil on each row opens a modal for name, code, client, location, dates, accent colour and status. This is the authoritative rename for the hub, fixing renames that previously never took root: projects created through the hub had no branding row, so the in-app rename silently updated nothing. Branding rows are now backfilled for every project, seeded automatically for future projects, and the branding save itself inserts the row if it is somehow still missing

A sync option on Edit Project keeps the in-app branding name (tab title, location-code prefix, report headers) aligned to the project code, so the hub name and the in-app name cannot drift apart again; untick it for a project that deliberately brands itself differently inside the app

Projects can now be archived (or put on hold) from Edit Project: archived projects disappear from Home, the switcher, the snapshots and Analytics, and sit greyed out on the Projects page behind a Show Archived toggle. Nothing is deleted and direct entry still works

New Analytics tab on the hub for the owner and platform supers: portfolio KPI tiles (weighted PPC over committed work due to date, using exactly the sidebar PPC formula per project; completion; overdue; open commitments), a project comparison table with a health badge (On Track, Watch, At Risk from the overdue share of open work), Status Mix and Overdue charts per project, portfolio-wide activity by company, and a multi-sheet Excel download. Every tile drills to the projects behind the number, and every row deep-links straight into that project's full Analytics page
Project edits are recorded in the audit log (who, what, when), and project creation now writes an audit row too

Fix: the vendor logo on witness invitations rendered as a broken image box. Two faults compounded: the logo was embedded as an attachment inside the event creation payload, which Graph silently discards (attachments on events are only supported through their own endpoint), so the body's inline reference pointed at nothing; and even a late attachment could never fix the email, because Exchange dispatches the invitations the moment an event is created with attendees. The send is now the Microsoft-documented three-step order: create the event silently with no attendees, attach the logo through the supported endpoint, then add the attendees, which is the step that sends the meeting requests with the logo aboard

The Test To Me logo verdict now tells the truth: it previously reported embedded whenever the creation call succeeded, which is exactly how a broken logo shipped unnoticed; it now reports embedded only when the attachment call itself succeeded. If the attachment is refused, the body is downgraded to the name-only variant before anyone is invited, so an inline reference can never again point at a missing attachment; if inviting the attendees fails, the orphan calendar entry is deleted and the error surfaces, leaving nothing behind

Events without a vendor logo keep the original single-call send. Meeting updates and cancellations are unchanged, and the digest emails were never affected: mail sends embed inline attachments in one supported call

Fix: the owner account was being treated as a client viewer, hiding the Witness Schedule's admin action column (Send, Update, Cancel, Test To Me) behind Request Invite buttons and hiding the drawer's new Test Invite To Me field. Root cause: the REV104 privilege resolver's owner blanket granted every key including Request Invites, which is not an elevated privilege but the client-viewer mode flag; combined with a company set on the owner's profile, the owner's session flipped into client-viewer mode. The owner now receives every elevated privilege and is never a client viewer; admins, supers, members and genuine client-company viewers are unchanged

Test Invite To Me is now available in the activity drawer itself: any admin, super or the owner opening a witness activity finds it on the Readiness tab under Witness Days, in every activity status, on any date, whatever the invite state. It creates the real event with the real template and vendor logo in your own calendar only, invites nobody, stamps nothing, and reports the result inline in the drawer

The drawer test deliberately uses the values currently on screen, including unsaved changes, so you can preview an edit before saving or sending anything; the button says so. It is also deliberately independent of the plan lock and of the invites privilege, because a self-only test changes nothing: the option is always there for admins and the owner, which is the point
The Witness Schedule's own Test To Me is unchanged and already appears in every invite state; the drawer covers the cases the schedule cannot, such as activities outside the selected period

Admin update emails work again for owner and super accounts. The digest claim table and several older policies were guarded by the legacy is_admin() database helper, which only recognised the old profile role; the REV104 owner/super rework moved accounts to the new role model and the helper started refusing them, blocking sends and hiding the send history (both lines read never sent). A one-file SQL migration redefines the helper as a strict superset: legacy profile admins keep access, and platform supers, the owner and per-project admins are recognised. This also repairs the audit log read the digest content is assembled from, and every other legacy policy referencing the helper
